If yes, map a non-threatening entry point — shared goals, questions rather than corrections, personal story over statistics — that doesn't trigger identity-protective reasoning.
models.before_after: I'll just show more data until the evidence is undeniable → More evidence is making this patient more defensive because the belief is identity-linked; I need to build trust and find common ground before the data can land.

models.name: Affect Heuristic
models.one_liner: We use our current emotional state as information when making judgments, conflating 'how I feel about this' with 'what the evidence says.'
models.problem: Emotional responses to risk (fear, disgust, enthusiasm) override analytical assessment of actual probabilities.
models.how_it_works: When evaluating risks or benefits, people substitute the easier question 'how do I feel about this?' for the harder 'what are the statistics?' High fear → overestimate risk; high enthusiasm → underestimate risk. Attractive things seem safer; disgusting things seem more dangerous than they are.
models.origin: Paul Slovic, 2002; building on Kahneman's dual-process framework.
models.scenario: A venture capitalist is evaluating two deals: a climate-tech startup with a charismatic, inspiring founder she met over dinner, and a B2B logistics software company with a quiet, methodical founder she found slightly awkward.
models.prompt: Separate your emotional response to each founder from the deal fundamentals. Score both companies on the same five criteria — market size, unit economics, team execution history, competitive moat, capital efficiency — before letting your personal reaction to either founder influence the decision.
models.before_after: The climate founder was electric and I feel genuinely excited about this one → My excitement about the climate founder is an emotional signal, not a financial signal; I need to score both deals on the same objective criteria before my feelings influence my check.

models.name: Curse of Knowledge
models.one_liner: Experts cannot imagine not knowing what they know, making it hard for them to communicate with or understand novices.
models.problem: Produces bad teaching, poor documentation, and communication failures between experts and non-experts.
models.how_it_works: Once information is in memory, it's nearly impossible to mentally simulate not having it. The expert assumes the listener has the same contextual background, uses jargon freely, and skips 'obvious' steps. The Feynman Technique directly counters this by forcing simple-language explanation.
models.origin: Chip Heath and Dan Heath in 'Made to Stick' (2006); underlying psychology studied earlier by Keysar et al.
models.scenario: A senior data scientist is presenting a new fraud detection model to the company's finance leadership team. His slides are full of AUC curves, precision-recall tradeoffs, and F1 scores, and the room looks blank and confused.
models.prompt: Imagine explaining this to a finance director who has never taken a statistics course. Replace every technical term with a business outcome: instead of 'AUC of 0.94,' say 'catches 94% of fraud before it costs us money.' List the three things your audience actually needs to decide — then rebuild your slides around only those three things.
models.before_after: I'll walk through the model metrics so they understand the technical rigor → These executives need to know if it's better than the current system, what it costs to run, and what decisions to make — not how we trained it.

models.name: IKEA Effect
models.one_liner: We place disproportionately high value on things we partially created ourselves.
models.problem: Overvaluation of in-house solutions, NIH ('not invented here') syndrome, and resistance to outsourcing.
models.how_it_works: Labor investment creates psychological ownership. Even when the output is objectively worse, creators value it far more than neutral observers. Partially explains why homemade food tastes better and why founders overvalue their companies. Labor without completion eliminates the effect — the build must finish.
models.origin: Michael Norton, Daniel Mochon, and Dan Ariely, 2011.
models.scenario: A product team built their own internal project management tool from scratch over six months. An off-the-shelf tool like Linear would cost $500/month and offer 10x the features, but the team insists their homegrown system is better and refuses to switch.
models.prompt: Ask a neutral person who didn't build the tool to compare both options on the same five criteria: time to create tasks, reporting quality, onboarding time for new hires, integration support, and maintenance burden. Then ask the team to score the homegrown tool as if they had bought it from a vendor — would they buy it at this quality?
models.before_after: Our internal tool is exactly what we need and we built it ourselves so it fits our workflow perfectly → A neutral comparison shows the external tool wins on four of five criteria; our strong preference for the internal tool is driven by labor investment, not objective quality.

models.name: Zeigarnik Effect
models.one_liner: Incomplete or interrupted tasks are remembered better than completed ones.
models.problem: Unfinished business creates persistent cognitive intrusion; can be harnessed to maintain engagement or create anxiety.
models.how_it_works: The brain maintains 'open loops' for unresolved tasks, keeping them active in working memory to drive completion. Waiters remember unpaid tabs perfectly but forget them once paid. Used in cliffhangers, to-do lists, and sales techniques. Close open loops to reduce mental load; deliberately leave them open to sustain motivation.
models.origin: Bluma Zeigarnik, 1927, based on observation by Kurt Lewin.
models.scenario: A novelist finds she can't stop thinking about her current chapter while trying to be present during family dinner — the unfinished scene keeps intruding into her thoughts.
models.prompt: Before closing the laptop, spend two minutes writing a specific next sentence or a 'parking note' — exactly where the scene is going next — to close the open cognitive loop. Then test whether the intrusive thoughts diminish at dinner.
models.before_after: I'll just stop mid-chapter and pick it up tomorrow → If I write a specific next-step note before closing my laptop, my brain can release the open loop instead of rehearsing it all evening.

models.name: Ben Franklin Effect
models.one_liner: Doing someone a favor makes you like them more — not less — because your brain rationalizes the effort as justified by liking them.
models.problem: Counter-intuitive persuasion dynamic: asking favors can build rapport better than granting them.
models.how_it_works: Cognitive dissonance: after doing a favor, the brain asks 'why did I help this person?' and answers 'because I like them' — strengthening positive feelings. Dissonance reduction makes the brain construct a narrative to justify the behavior. Franklin used this deliberately to convert a political opponent by asking to borrow a rare book.
models.origin: Named after Benjamin Franklin's autobiography account; studied by Jecker and Landy, 1969.
models.scenario: A new manager is trying to build rapport with a skeptical senior engineer who is politically influential on the team and visibly cool toward her.
models.prompt: Identify one specific small favor to ask the engineer — advice on a technical decision, reviewing a doc in his area of expertise, or input on a team process — that is genuine, not manufactured. Ask sincerely, then observe whether his attitude warms over the following two weeks.
models.before_after: I need to impress him by giving him great resources and doing things for him → Asking him for advice on a real problem gives him the chance to invest in helping me, which tends to increase his positive feelings toward me more than me giving things to him.

models.name: Serial Position Effect
models.one_liner: Items at the beginning and end of a list are remembered better than those in the middle.
models.problem: Distorts evaluation of candidates (interview order matters), evidence sequences, and any information presented serially.
models.how_it_works: Primacy effect: early items encoded into long-term memory with more rehearsal. Recency effect: late items still in working memory at recall. Middle items receive neither advantage. Produces a U-shaped recall curve. Implications: put key messages first or last; never bury important information in the middle.
models.origin: Ebbinghaus (1885); experimentally validated by Murdock (1962) and Glanzer & Cunitz (1966).
models.scenario: A consultant is pitching a board of directors on a restructuring proposal that involves seven key recommendations. She plans to present them in chronological implementation order, which puts the highest-impact recommendations in the middle of the deck.
models.prompt: Reorder your presentation so the most critical recommendation is first and the strongest call-to-action is last. Move supporting detail and lower-priority items to the middle. Then ask: if the board remembers only items one and seven, will they walk out with the right impression?
models.before_after: I'll present the recommendations in the order we'd implement them, which flows logically → Board members will remember the first and last points most clearly, so I need my highest-stakes recommendation to open and my clearest ask to close.

models.name: False Consensus Effect
models.one_liner: We overestimate how many other people share our beliefs, preferences, and behaviors.
models.problem: Leads to miscalibrated product design, political blind spots, and assuming your minority view is the majority view.
models.how_it_works: We generalize from our own experience and social circle to the broader population. People project their own opinions onto others, assuming consensus where none exists. Especially strong for attitudes where we feel we are 'obviously correct.' Surveys and direct user research counter it.
models.origin: Lee Ross, David Greene, and Pamela House, 1977.
models.scenario: A product manager at a fintech company is designing an onboarding flow. Based on her own experience and conversations with three colleagues, she assumes most users will immediately connect their bank account when prompted.
models.prompt: Before finalizing the design, write down your assumption ('X% of users will do Y') and identify exactly how many real users outside your team you have spoken to. Then run a five-person usability test with actual target users to check whether your assumed behavior matches theirs.
models.before_after: Everyone I've talked to would connect their bank immediately, so we'll design the whole flow around that → My 'everyone' is three colleagues who work in fintech — I need to test this with five real target users before assuming their behavior matches mine.

models.name: Negativity Bias
models.one_liner: Negative events, stimuli, and emotions have greater psychological impact than positive ones of equivalent magnitude.
models.problem: Distorts risk assessment, relationship quality, feedback processing, and news consumption patterns.
models.how_it_works: Evolutionary origins: losses (predators, poisons) carried higher fitness costs than equivalent gains. The brain weighs threats more heavily than opportunities. One bad review hurts more than one good review helps. Peak-end rule amplifies this: negative peaks are remembered more vividly. Deliberately tracking and amplifying positives counters chronic negativity bias.
models.origin: Paul Rozin and Edward Royzman, 2001; earlier evolutionary psychology literature.
models.scenario: A restaurant owner reads 47 five-star reviews and one scathing two-star review on Monday morning. He spends the entire day spiraling about the one bad review, forwarding it to his team repeatedly, while the 47 positive reviews fade from his mind.
models.prompt: Before responding to the negative review, calculate the actual ratio: how many positive reviews were posted versus negative ones this month? Then write down three specific things the positive reviews praised that you want to preserve and build on. Address the negative review last, with that context in place.
models.before_after: This one bad review could destroy us — I need to fix everything it mentions immediately → One negative review among 47 positives represents a 2% dissatisfaction rate; I should address it calmly while making sure the 98% of things working well don't get disrupted.

models.name: Optimism Bias
models.one_liner: We believe we are less likely than average to experience negative events and more likely to experience positive ones.
models.problem: Drives poor risk management, underinsurance, over-leveraged investments, and reckless behavior.
models.how_it_works: Most people rate themselves as above-average drivers, healthier than peers, and less likely to get divorced — statistically impossible. The brain preferentially processes positive self-relevant information. Tali Sharot's research shows it's neurologically hardwired. Steelmanning downside scenarios and pre-mortems help calibrate.
models.origin: Neil Weinstein, 1980; Tali Sharot's neuroscience research, 2011.
models.scenario: A first-time entrepreneur is preparing to leave her stable job in two weeks to launch a consumer app, projecting she'll reach profitability within eight months based on a best-case user growth scenario.
models.prompt: Run a pre-mortem: imagine it is 18 months from now and the venture has failed. Write the five most plausible reasons it failed — not black swans, but normal startup failure modes. Then ask: does my current plan address any of these, and how much runway do I actually need to survive them?
models.before_after: I'll hit profitability in eight months if everything goes according to plan → Most consumer apps take 18–24 months to find product-market fit; if I write out the five ways this fails normally, I realize I need 18 months of runway, not eight.

models.name: Authority Bias
models.one_liner: We attribute greater accuracy and trustworthiness to the opinions of authority figures.
models.problem: Leads to uncritical deference to credentials, titles, and confident speakers even when their claims are wrong.
models.how_it_works: Milgram's obedience experiments showed most people would administer apparently fatal shocks when instructed by an authority figure. In everyday contexts, lab coats, titles, and confident delivery amplify perceived credibility. Evaluating arguments on their merits, not the source's status, counters it.
models.origin: Studied by Milgram (1963); Cialdini's 'Influence' (1984) codified it as a persuasion principle.
models.scenario: During a hospital case conference, the attending physician confidently states a diagnosis. Three residents disagree based on lab results they reviewed, but nobody speaks up because the attending is the most senior person in the room.
models.prompt: Separate the argument from the arguer: write down the specific clinical evidence that supports the attending's diagnosis and the specific evidence that contradicts it. Then evaluate which interpretation the data supports, independent of who is making each claim.
models.before_after: The attending said it's X, so it must be X — he's been doing this for 20 years → The attending's 20 years of experience is relevant context, but these three lab results are objective data that needs to be addressed regardless of who's in the room.

models.name: Mere Exposure Effect
models.one_liner: Repeated exposure to a stimulus increases our liking of it, regardless of its objective qualities.
models.problem: Breeds false familiarity in advertising, politics, and social relationships — frequency displaces quality.
models.how_it_works: Even subliminal repeated exposure to faces, words, or symbols increases positive affect toward them. Explains why repeated advertising works, why familiar names win elections, and why we tend to like people we see often. Awareness of the effect and seeking objective quality criteria help counteract it.
models.origin: Robert Zajonc, 1968.
models.scenario: A hiring manager is reviewing four candidates for a senior engineering role. She has met candidates A and B twice each in video calls and reviewed their work samples once. She has only seen candidates C and D's written applications. She feels more confident about A and B without being able to articulate why.
models.prompt: Score all four candidates on the same written rubric — technical depth, communication clarity, relevant experience, problem-solving evidence — before your next conversation with any of them. Ask: is my higher confidence in A and B driven by their actual qualifications, or by the number of times I've seen their faces?
models.before_after: A and B feel like stronger candidates — I just have a better sense of who they are → I've seen A and B more often, which increases familiarity and therefore liking; I need to score all four on the same criteria before familiarity distorts my ranking.

models.name: Illusory Truth Effect
models.one_liner: Repeated statements are judged as more true than novel statements, regardless of their actual accuracy.
models.problem: Misinformation and propaganda become more believable through sheer repetition.
models.how_it_works: Processing fluency acts as a truth proxy: familiar information is processed faster, and that fluency is misattributed to truthfulness. Works even when people initially know the statement is false and even when told to ignore familiarity. The antidote is actively evaluating claims against evidence rather than relying on familiarity.
models.origin: Hasher, Goldstein, and Toppino, 1977; more extensively studied since 2015.
models.scenario: A marketing team has been repeating the internal claim that 'our platform reduces customer churn by 40%' in all-hands meetings, investor decks, and sales calls for eight months. New employees take it as established fact, but no one can trace it back to a specific study.
models.prompt: Trace this claim to its original source: who first stated it, what data was it based on, and when was it measured? If you cannot produce the methodology within five minutes, treat it as unverified and label it as 'claimed' rather than 'proven' until you can replicate the finding.
models.before_after: Our platform reduces churn by 40% — we've been saying that for months so it must be true → We've repeated this claim so many times it feels solid, but I need to find the original data source before we put it in an investor deck.

models.name: Representativeness Heuristic
models.one_liner: We judge probability by how closely something resembles our mental prototype, ignoring base rates.
models.problem: Produces stereotype-based judgments, ignores statistical base rates, and causes the conjunction fallacy.
models.how_it_works: When told 'Linda is a feminist bank teller,' people rate this as more probable than 'Linda is a bank teller' — a logical impossibility explained by Linda matching the feminist prototype better. Base rate neglect: we ignore that most people are not bank tellers, let alone feminist bank tellers. System 1 pattern-matches to prototypes rather than computing probabilities.
models.origin: Amos Tversky and Daniel Kahneman, 1974.
models.scenario: A venture capitalist passes on a seed-stage fintech company because the two founders are recent PhD graduates with no prior startup experience, even though the market data and product traction are exceptionally strong.
models.prompt: Write down the base rate: what percentage of successful VC-backed companies had first-time founders? Then separately evaluate this company's traction metrics as if the founders fit your prototype of a 'successful founder.' Ask: am I judging the evidence, or am I judging how well these founders match my mental template?
models.before_after: These founders don't look like the successful founders I've backed before, so I'm going to pass → Most of my successful bets had non-obvious founders; I need to evaluate their traction data on its own merits rather than how closely they match my prototype.

models.name: System 1 / System 2 Thinking
models.one_liner: Two modes of thought: fast/automatic/intuitive (System 1) vs. slow/deliberate/analytical (System 2) — most biases arise from System 1 being applied where System 2 is needed.
models.problem: Cognitive errors arise when fast, automatic thinking handles situations requiring careful analysis.
models.how_it_works: System 1 operates automatically, quickly, effortlessly, associatively, and emotionally. System 2 is slow, deliberate, effortful, and rule-governed. System 1 generates impressions; System 2 endorses or overrides them. Under cognitive load, time pressure, or distraction, System 2 disengages and System 1 dominates. Most heuristics and biases are System 1 shortcuts.
models.origin: Stanovich and West, 2000; popularized by Daniel Kahneman, 'Thinking, Fast and Slow,' 2011.
models.scenario: A seasoned ER doctor must decide within 60 seconds whether a patient's chest pain is anxiety or a heart attack — the patient is young, fit, and calm, which 'doesn't look like' a cardiac case.
models.prompt: Ask: 'Am I reacting to a vivid pattern (System 1) or actually running the diagnostic checklist (System 2)?' Then deliberately slow down and list three alternative diagnoses you would miss if you trust the first impression.
models.before_after: The doctor discharges the patient because he doesn't fit the profile and the impression feels right → The doctor recognizes the 'doesn't look sick' feeling as System 1 and runs the ECG protocol anyway, catching a STEMI.

models.name: Ingroup Bias
models.one_liner: We favor members of groups we belong to over outsiders, often unconsciously.
models.problem: Drives tribalism, nepotism, discrimination, and poor resource allocation in organizations.
models.how_it_works: Merely categorizing people into groups (even arbitrary ones, as in Tajfel's minimal group experiments) produces favoritism toward the ingroup. We perceive ingroup members as more competent, trustworthy, and deserving. The bias operates even when people sincerely believe they are impartial.
models.origin: Henri Tajfel and John Turner, Social Identity Theory, 1979.
models.scenario: A hiring manager at a Series B startup is choosing between two equally qualified candidates — one is a former colleague she worked with closely, the other is a stranger from a different industry background.
models.prompt: List the objective criteria you used to evaluate both candidates. For each criterion, ask: 'Would I have scored this the same way if I had never met either person?' Flag any criterion where the answer is 'probably not' and reassess those scores with the stranger in mind.
models.before_after: The manager hires the former colleague because she 'just felt more confident' in him → The manager spots that she weighted 'culture fit' heavily only for the stranger, equalizes the assessment, and makes the hire on actual skill evidence.

models.name: Feynman Technique
models.one_liner: Expose gaps in your understanding by attempting to explain a concept in plain language, as if teaching a child.
models.problem: People mistake recognition (reading notes and feeling familiar) for actual understanding.
models.how_it_works: Four steps: (1) choose a topic; (2) explain it in simple language without jargon; (3) identify where the explanation breaks down or becomes vague; (4) return to source material to fill those gaps, then re-explain. The breakdown points are precisely where real learning is needed. Combats the illusion of competence.
models.origin: Named after physicist Richard Feynman (1918–1988); not formally published by him but derived from his learning practices.
models.scenario: A product manager has attended three workshops on machine learning and writes 'understands ML' on her resume, but can't clearly explain to her engineering team why their model is overfitting.
models.prompt: Close every tab and write a one-page explanation of overfitting as if you're teaching a 12-year-old who has never coded. Every sentence where you reach for jargon or feel vague is a gap — circle those sentences, then go back to the source material specifically for those points.
models.before_after: The PM says 'I know what overfitting is' because she can recognize the word → After trying to explain it simply, she discovers she cannot describe why more data helps, and goes back to learn that specific piece.

models.name: Spacing Effect (Spaced Repetition)
models.one_liner: Distributing study sessions over time produces far better long-term retention than massing them (cramming).
models.problem: Cramming produces rapid forgetting; students mistake short-term fluency for durable learning.
models.how_it_works: Ebbinghaus's forgetting curve shows memory decays exponentially after learning. Reviewing just before the point of forgetting (expanding intervals: 1 day, 3 days, 1 week, 1 month…) strengthens the memory trace most efficiently. Spaced repetition systems (Anki, SuperMemo) automate scheduling. Combined with retrieval practice (testing), retention improves 150%+ vs. re-reading.
models.origin: Hermann Ebbinghaus, 1885; Sebastian Leitner's flashcard system, 1972; Piotr Wozniak's SuperMemo algorithm, 1987.
models.scenario: A medical student has a pharmacology exam in two weeks and plans to spend the last three days memorizing 300 drug interactions in one intensive session.
models.prompt: Map out your two weeks as a calendar. Instead of massing all review at the end, schedule the first pass today, a second pass in 3 days, a third in 7 days, and a final review the day before. For each session, ask: 'Am I reviewing what I'm about to forget — not what I still remember easily?'
models.before_after: The student crams the weekend before, feels confident Monday, then blanks on Wednesday's exam → The student spreads reviews across two weeks and retains 80% of the drug interactions because each review fires just as the memory is fading.

models.name: Interleaving
models.one_liner: Mixing different topics or problem types during practice produces better learning than drilling one type at a time.
models.problem: Blocked practice (all of type A, then all of type B) feels productive but produces fragile, context-dependent learning.
models.how_it_works: Interleaved practice forces the brain to retrieve and re-apply the right strategy for each problem type, strengthening discrimination. The 'desirable difficulty' of switching makes practice feel harder but produces superior long-term retention and transfer. Distinct from spacing: interleaving fills rest periods with different learning material.
models.origin: Robert Bjork's 'desirable difficulties' research; Rohrer and Taylor, 2007.
models.scenario: A high school math teacher designs a unit on quadratic equations where students spend Monday mastering factoring, Tuesday on the quadratic formula, and Wednesday on completing the square — drilling one method per day.
models.prompt: Redesign one practice set so that each problem could require any of the three methods. After students attempt it, ask them: 'How did you decide which method to use?' — that metacognitive step is where the real learning lives.
models.before_after: Students ace the factoring quiz on Monday but struggle on Friday's mixed-method test because they've never had to choose → Students who practiced interleaved sets perform 30% better on transfer tests because they've learned to recognize which tool fits the problem.

models.name: Deliberate Practice
models.one_liner: Expert performance is built through focused, feedback-driven practice on specific weaknesses — not mere accumulated hours.
models.problem: Time-on-task alone does not produce expertise; most 'practice' is mindless repetition that plateaus quickly.
models.how_it_works: Five components: (1) specific goal just beyond current ability; (2) full concentration; (3) immediate feedback; (4) reflection and error correction; (5) mental representation building. Not inherently enjoyable — it's cognitively demanding. Gladwell's '10,000 hours' misreads Ericsson: quality and structure of practice matter far more than quantity.
models.origin: K. Anders Ericsson, 'The Role of Deliberate Practice in the Acquisition of Expert Performance,' 1993; book 'Peak,' 2016.
models.scenario: A junior software engineer puts in 10-hour days coding features but hasn't improved her system design skills in two years — she's busy but stuck at the same level.
models.prompt: Identify the single weakest sub-skill in your work (e.g., 'I can't design a scalable caching layer without looking it up'). Design one focused 45-minute session this week targeting only that gap: find a harder-than-comfortable exercise, attempt it without hints, then compare to an expert solution and analyze every difference.
models.before_after: The engineer clocks hours shipping features and assumes she's growing → She realizes she's been repeating comfortable work and carves out weekly deliberate sessions on design patterns, closing real skill gaps in three months.

models.name: Growth Mindset
models.one_liner: The belief that abilities and intelligence are developable through effort, strategy, and learning — not fixed traits.
models.problem: Fixed mindset causes people to avoid challenges, give up at setbacks, and interpret failure as evidence of inadequacy.
models.how_it_works: Fixed mindset: every situation is a judgment on your innate ability → avoidance, defensiveness, blame. Growth mindset: every challenge is information about what to practice → persistence, feedback-seeking, resilience. Praise process ('you worked hard') over outcome ('you're smart') to cultivate it. Neural plasticity is the scientific foundation.
models.origin: Carol Dweck, Stanford University; 'Mindset: The New Psychology of Success,' 2006.
models.scenario: A first-time startup founder gets brutally negative feedback from his first investor pitch — the investors said his market sizing was 'embarrassingly wrong' — and he is considering shelving the idea.
models.prompt: Reframe the feedback as a specific map of what to learn next. Ask: 'What exact skill or knowledge was revealed as a gap here — market research, financial modeling, or pitch storytelling?' Then identify the fastest way to close that one gap before the next pitch.
models.before_after: The founder interprets 'embarrassingly wrong' as proof he's not cut out for this and gives up → The founder identifies market sizing as a learnable skill, spends a week studying comparable analyses, and returns with a credible TAM/SAM/SOM breakdown.

models.name: Bloom's Taxonomy
models.one_liner: A six-level hierarchy of cognitive skills, from rote recall at the bottom to creative synthesis at the top.
models.problem: Education and training often stops at recall, producing learners who can recite facts but cannot apply or evaluate them.
models.how_it_works: Six levels in ascending order: Remember → Understand → Apply → Analyze → Evaluate → Create. Lower levels are prerequisites for higher ones. Effective learning objectives and assessments should target the highest relevant level, not default to recall. Revised by Krathwohl in 2001 to add the 'Create' level.
models.origin: Benjamin Bloom and committee, 1956; revised by Lorin Anderson and David Krathwohl, 2001.
models.scenario: A corporate L&D team designs a cybersecurity training course where employees watch videos and answer multiple-choice 'what is phishing?' questions — and then still click phishing links at the same rate as before.
models.prompt: Map your current training activities to the six Bloom levels. Then ask: 'At what level does the real job demand operate?' If the job requires employees to Evaluate a suspicious email in real time, redesign the assessment to match — use simulated phishing attempts, not recall quizzes.
models.before_after: The L&D team measures 95% quiz pass rates and declares success → After mapping to Bloom, they realize the course only tests Remember, while the job demands Apply and Evaluate, so they redesign with live-scenario drills.

models.name: Kolb's Learning Cycle
models.one_liner: Learning requires cycling through four stages: concrete experience, reflection, abstract conceptualization, and active experimentation.
models.problem: People often skip stages (reflect but never experiment; act but never reflect), producing incomplete or fragile learning.
models.how_it_works: Four stages: (1) Concrete Experience — do something; (2) Reflective Observation — analyze what happened; (3) Abstract Conceptualization — build/update mental model; (4) Active Experimentation — test the new model. Entry point varies by learning style; full cycle is required for deep learning. Organizations often skip reflection under time pressure.
models.origin: David Kolb, 1984, drawing on Dewey, Piaget, and Lewin.
models.scenario: An engineering team ships a feature that caused a production outage, holds a 10-minute post-mortem, and immediately moves on to the next sprint without updating any processes.
models.prompt: Schedule a 30-minute structured retrospective that explicitly moves through all four stages: (1) What concretely happened? (2) What patterns do we notice when we step back? (3) What mental model or assumption was wrong? (4) What specific experiment will we run in the next sprint to test the new model?
models.before_after: The team discusses the outage briefly and concludes 'we need to be more careful,' then repeats the same mistake two sprints later → The team surfaces the faulty assumption about cache invalidation, updates their deployment checklist, and the failure class never recurs.

models.name: Zone of Proximal Development (ZPD)
models.one_liner: Learning happens optimally in the zone between what you can do alone and what you can do with expert guidance.
models.problem: Tasks too easy produce no growth; tasks too hard produce frustration and failure. Optimal challenge is rarely calibrated.
models.how_it_works: ZPD is the gap between current independent capability and potential capability with scaffolding (expert guidance, tools, peers). The 'scaffolding' metaphor: provide just enough support to reach the next level, then withdraw it to consolidate independence. Deliberate practice and stretch goals operationalize the ZPD concept in adult learning.
models.origin: Lev Vygotsky, 1930s (published posthumously); major influence on educational scaffolding theory.
models.scenario: A junior data analyst is assigned solo to build an executive dashboard — a task well beyond her current skills — while her senior colleague gets assigned repetitive report formatting that takes 10 minutes.
models.prompt: Assess the gap between what the junior analyst can do independently and what she can do with guidance. Then redesign the assignment: pair her with the senior on the dashboard for the first iteration, have the senior narrate decisions out loud, and let the junior lead the second iteration with the senior available for questions only.
models.before_after: The junior analyst either fails alone and loses confidence, or the senior takes over and she learns nothing → Structured scaffolding lets the junior analyst stretch just past her current edge and build a genuine mental model of dashboard design.

models.name: Double-Loop Learning
models.one_liner: Beyond fixing errors in action (single-loop), question the underlying assumptions and values that generated the action.
models.problem: Organizations and individuals keep repeating the same mistakes because they fix symptoms without examining the faulty mental models causing them.
models.how_it_works: Single-loop: detect error → adjust behavior → repeat. Thermostat analogy: it adjusts heat but never questions whether the target temperature is right. Double-loop: detect error → question governing assumptions → update mental model → change behavior from a new foundation. Requires psychological safety; threatened people revert to single-loop defensiveness.
models.origin: Chris Argyris, Harvard Business Review, 1977; 'Teaching Smart People How to Learn,' 1991.
models.scenario: A sales director notices her team misses quota every Q4 and responds each year by pushing harder — more calls, more incentives, extended hours — with no lasting improvement.
models.prompt: Instead of asking 'How do we fix Q4 performance?' ask 'What assumption is embedded in our Q4 strategy that keeps producing this outcome?' Map the governing beliefs (e.g., 'activity drives results' or 'customers buy on price') and test whether any of those beliefs are the actual root cause.
models.before_after: The director doubles the call quota every Q4 and gets the same 12% miss → After questioning the assumption that call volume drives Q4 sales, she discovers enterprise customers freeze budgets in November and redesigns the pipeline strategy around Q3 close dates.

models.name: Learning Pyramid / Cone of Experience
models.one_liner: Active learning methods (teaching others, practice) produce dramatically higher retention than passive methods (lecture, reading).
models.problem: Traditional education over-relies on passive delivery methods with low retention.
models.how_it_works: Edgar Dale's original 1946 Cone of Experience classified media by abstractness (verbal → real experience). Later organizations added specific retention percentages (lecture 5%, teaching others 90%) that are NOT scientifically validated. The directional principle — active beats passive — IS well-supported. The specific percentages are educational mythology. Use as a directional heuristic, not as precise data.
models.origin: Edgar Dale, 1946; specific percentages popularized but not validated by NTL Institute.
models.scenario: A compliance officer rolls out mandatory anti-harassment training via a 45-minute lecture video and a knowledge-check quiz, then is surprised when behavioral incidents don't decrease.
models.prompt: Ask: 'What percentage of this training is passive (watching, listening, reading) versus active (role-play, teaching, applying)?' Then redesign so that at least one learning activity requires employees to produce an output — explain the policy to a peer, handle a simulated scenario, or teach back one section.
models.before_after: The compliance officer assumes employees learned because they passed the quiz → After adding peer role-play scenarios, employees can actually navigate ambiguous situations because they practiced doing it, not just recognizing it.

models.name: Ebbinghaus Forgetting Curve
models.one_liner: Without reinforcement, memory decays exponentially — most forgetting happens within hours of learning.
models.problem: Learners are surprised by how much they forget; study strategies don't account for memory decay.
models.how_it_works: Ebbinghaus found retention drops ~56% within 1 hour, ~66% within 24 hours, and ~75% within 6 days of initial learning (for meaningless syllables; meaningful content decays more slowly). Each review 'resets and flattens' the curve. Spaced repetition uses this curve to schedule reviews at mathematically optimal intervals.
models.origin: Hermann Ebbinghaus, 'Über das Gedächtnis,' 1885.
models.scenario: A sales rep attends a two-day product training, takes detailed notes, and feels ready — but three weeks later can barely remember the feature differentiators when talking to a prospect.
models.prompt: Immediately after training, schedule three short review sessions: one tonight (5 minutes of recall without notes), one in 3 days, and one in 10 days. For each session, close your notes and write down every feature differentiator you can recall — then check what you missed. Those misses are the only things worth reviewing.
models.before_after: The sales rep relies on the feeling of fluency from the training week and doesn't review until before the next demo → By scheduling reviews at the forgetting curve's steepest points, she retains 85% of key differentiators three weeks out instead of 25%.

models.name: Retrieval Practice / Testing Effect
models.one_liner: Actively recalling information strengthens memory far more than passively re-reading it.
models.problem: Students default to re-reading (passive), which creates false fluency while producing poor long-term retention.
models.how_it_works: The act of retrieval — not the exposure — strengthens the memory trace. Even failed retrieval attempts (followed by correct answer) produce better long-term retention than re-study. Flashcards, practice tests, and 'close the book and write what you remember' all leverage this. Combined with spaced repetition, produces 150%+ retention advantage over re-reading.
models.origin: Cattell (1887) first noted it; Roediger and Karpicke, 2006, provided influential experimental evidence.
models.scenario: A law student preparing for the bar exam spends 80% of his study time re-reading case briefs and outlines, telling himself 'I just need to see it more times' when something feels unfamiliar.
models.prompt: For the next study session, close every resource and write from memory everything you know about the topic before opening any notes. Then compare what you wrote against the source — the gaps you find in your own recall are the exact items to study, not the items you already wrote correctly.
models.before_after: The student re-reads the contracts outline four times and feels familiar with it, then blanks on the consideration elements during the exam → After switching to active recall sessions, he immediately surfaces what he doesn't actually know versus what merely looks familiar.

models.name: Metacognition
models.one_liner: Thinking about your own thinking — monitoring and regulating your cognitive processes.
models.problem: Without metacognition, people don't notice when their understanding is illusory, their strategies are ineffective, or their biases are active.
models.how_it_works: Two components: (1) metacognitive knowledge — knowing about your own cognitive strengths, weaknesses, and strategies; (2) metacognitive regulation — monitoring comprehension in real time and adjusting strategy when understanding fails. High metacognitive skill correlates with academic achievement and professional expertise. Dunning-Kruger is fundamentally a metacognition failure.
models.origin: John Flavell coined the term in 1976; Vygotsky's work on inner speech is an antecedent.
models.scenario: A senior product manager confidently leads a roadmap planning session, makes quick decisions, and only realizes six weeks later that she misunderstood the technical constraints from the beginning — wasting two sprints.
models.prompt: Before your next decision-heavy meeting, take 5 minutes to write: 'What do I believe I understand well about this problem? What am I less sure about? What would change my conclusion?' Then, at the end of the meeting, ask one engineer: 'Did I seem to have an accurate picture of the technical constraints?' Use the gap between your self-assessment and their answer as calibration data.
models.before_after: The PM proceeds with full confidence because the plan 'makes sense,' not noticing she never verified her technical assumptions → Regular metacognitive check-ins reveal she consistently underestimates backend complexity, and she starts asking calibration questions before committing to timelines.

models.name: Focused vs. Diffuse Thinking
models.one_liner: Alternating between intense focused attention and relaxed unfocused mind produces better learning and creativity than sustained focus alone.
models.problem: People force continuous concentration, blocking the subconscious consolidation and insight that diffuse mode enables.
models.how_it_works: Focused mode: tight attention to a specific problem, activates known neural pathways. Diffuse mode: relaxed background processing, activates broader neural connections and produces insight. Switching is the key: study hard, then rest. Sleep, exercise, and shower are diffuse mode activators. Explains why solutions appear after stepping away.
models.origin: Popularized by Barbara Oakley in 'A Mind for Numbers,' 2014; based on neuroscience research on default mode network.
models.scenario: A novelist has been staring at the same plot problem for three hours — she knows her second act has a structural hole but the harder she concentrates, the more blocked she feels.
models.prompt: Set a 20-minute timer and deliberately stop working on the plot problem — go for a walk, take a shower, or do a completely unrelated mechanical task. Before you step away, write the problem statement in one sentence so it's loaded into working memory. When you return, write down the first idea that surfaces before evaluating it.
models.before_after: The novelist forces another hour of staring at the screen, generating nothing, and ends the day frustrated → After a 20-minute walk, the structural solution appears unbidden during the walk and she captures it immediately — the diffuse network made the connection focused mode couldn't.

models.name: What? So What? Now What?
models.one_liner: A three-stage reflective framework: describe what happened, extract its significance, then determine action.
models.problem: People reflect without structure, producing either shallow description or endless rumination without actionable output.
models.how_it_works: What? — observable facts, no interpretation. So What? — meaning, implications, lessons. Now What? — specific actions to take. Forces separation of observation from interpretation from decision. Applicable to individual debriefs, team retrospectives, and after-action reviews. Simple enough to use in real time.
models.origin: Adapted from John Driscoll's reflective practice model (1994); popularized in coaching and organizational learning.
models.scenario: A startup team's big product launch gets half the expected sign-ups and the founders spend the debrief arguing about whose fault it was, without reaching any useful conclusions.
models.prompt: Facilitate the debrief in three strict phases: (1) 'What?' — everyone states only observable facts, no interpretations allowed. (2) 'So What?' — what patterns or implications do these facts suggest? (3) 'Now What?' — what are three specific things we will do differently in the next launch? Write each phase on a separate section of the whiteboard and physically move between them.
models.before_after: The debrief devolves into a blame session about the email campaign timing and ends with vague commitments to 'communicate better' → The structured framework produces a specific finding: landing page conversion was 2%, not messaging volume, leading to a concrete A/B test for next week.

models.name: Munger's Latticework of Mental Models
models.one_liner: Build a diverse library of models from multiple disciplines and use them as lenses to analyze any problem.
models.problem: Specialists apply the same narrow framework to every problem ('to a man with a hammer, everything looks like a nail').
models.how_it_works: Actively collect 80–100 fundamental models from mathematics, physics, biology, psychology, economics, and history. When facing a problem, run it through multiple lenses. Convergent signals across models increase confidence; contradictions reveal complexity. The models don't give answers — they generate better questions.
models.origin: Charlie Munger, Vice Chairman of Berkshire Hathaway; 'Poor Charlie's Almanack' (2005).
models.scenario: A venture capitalist is evaluating a biotech startup and only analyzes it through a financial lens — revenue projections, burn rate, comparable exits — missing the regulatory and biology-layer risks.
models.prompt: Before finalizing your investment thesis, run the opportunity through at least four different disciplinary lenses: biology (is the science actually sound?), incentives (who benefits if this fails?), history (what happened to the last five companies with this regulatory profile?), and systems thinking (what second-order effects would FDA approval trigger on incumbents?). Note where multiple lenses converge versus contradict.
models.before_after: The VC approves the deal because the financials look strong → Running it through a regulatory history lens reveals that three identical FDA pathways failed in the past decade, and the biology lens surfaces an unresolved mechanism-of-action question that changes the risk profile entirely.

models.name: Base Rate Fallacy
models.one_liner: We ignore population-level statistics (base rates) when evaluating individual cases, focusing instead on vivid specific information.
models.problem: Leads to wildly miscalibrated probability assessments in medicine, investment, security, and hiring.
models.how_it_works: Given 'this person tested positive for a rare disease (1% prevalence) with a 95% accurate test,' most people guess ~95% chance the person has the disease. The correct answer is ~16% (Bayes' theorem accounts for the many false positives from the 99% without the disease). Representativeness heuristic crowds out base rates. Kahneman's 'outside view' (reference class forecasting) is the counter.
models.origin: Bar-Hillel, 1980; related to Kahneman and Tversky's representativeness heuristic research.
models.scenario: A hiring manager reads a glowing recommendation letter for a candidate and becomes 90% confident the person will be a top performer — not knowing that the person who wrote the letter praises everyone.
models.prompt: Before updating your confidence based on the vivid individual evidence (the letter, the interview), ask: 'What is the base rate for this category?' How often do candidates with this profile actually become top performers in your company's data? Anchor on that number first, then adjust for the specific evidence.
models.before_after: The hiring manager feels highly confident because the letter is compelling and the interview went well → After checking that only 20% of hires at this level reach 'top performer' status within a year, she recalibrates her confidence to a realistic starting point and asks for a work sample test.

models.name: Conjunction Fallacy
models.one_liner: We judge a specific, detailed scenario as more probable than a broader, less specific one that contains it.
models.problem: Vivid, representative details make implausible combinations feel more likely than simple possibilities.
models.how_it_works: Classic example: 'Linda is a feminist bank teller' rated as more probable than 'Linda is a bank teller,' despite the latter being the logical superset. The conjunction (A AND B) can never be more probable than either A or B alone. The representativeness heuristic overrides this logic because Linda matches the feminist prototype more strongly.
models.origin: Amos Tversky and Daniel Kahneman, 1983.
models.scenario: A product team is deciding which risk is more likely: 'the launch will fail' versus 'the launch will fail because the marketing campaign underdelivers during a holiday week.' Team members rate the second scenario as more concerning.
models.prompt: Whenever you find yourself more worried about a detailed scenario than a general one, stop and ask: 'Is this specific scenario a subset of the general risk or a completely separate risk?' If it's a subset, the general risk must be at least as probable. Strip out the vivid details and re-evaluate the base probability of the simpler, broader failure mode.
models.before_after: The team allocates all their contingency budget to the holiday-week marketing risk because the story feels concrete and plausible → After applying the conjunction test, they realize 'launch fails' encompasses a dozen failure modes and they diversify their contingency across the highest-impact categories instead.

models.name: Hot-Hand Fallacy
models.one_liner: After a streak of successes, we believe the winning streak will continue due to some persistent state — even in random processes.
models.problem: Mispredicts performance continuation, leads to overinvestment in 'hot' stocks, athletes, and strategies.
models.how_it_works: Opposite of the gambler's fallacy but same root: pattern-seeking in randomness. We perceive non-existent momentum. Originally studied in basketball (players 'on fire'), Gilovich et al. (1985) found no statistical hot hand. Reanalysis with corrected methodology (Miller and Sanjurjo, 2018) found a small real hot-hand effect, making this a partially contested finding.
models.origin: Thomas Gilovich, Robert Vallone, and Amos Tversky, 1985.
models.scenario: A hedge fund manager has had three winning trades in a row and decides to double position size on a fourth trade, convinced the analyst is 'on a roll' and the streak will continue.
models.prompt: List the actual statistical base rate for this outcome, then ask: if you removed the last three results from your memory, would you still make this bet at this size?
models.before_after: The analyst has been right three times in a row, so I should trust their conviction and go bigger on this one → The last three wins change the probability of the next outcome by exactly zero; I need to evaluate this trade on its own merits, not on streak momentum.

models.name: Impact Bias
models.one_liner: We overestimate how intensely and how long future emotional events (good or bad) will affect us.
models.problem: Drives over-pursuit of goals whose achievement disappoints, and excessive fear of setbacks that are actually manageable.
models.how_it_works: Focalism: when imagining a future event, we zoom in on it and ignore the many other things that will fill our attention and moderate our reaction. 'Psychological immune system' — resilience mechanisms we don't anticipate — also attenuates emotional impact. Getting a promotion or losing a job rarely changes long-term happiness as much as predicted.
models.origin: Daniel Gilbert and Timothy Wilson, 2000; 'affective forecasting' research.
models.scenario: A product manager is paralyzed with anxiety about a potential product launch failure, convinced it will derail their career and leave them miserable for years.
models.prompt: Imagine it is 18 months after the worst-case outcome has occurred — list five things that will be filling your attention and daily life at that point, and estimate how much of each day you will still be thinking about this launch.
models.before_after: If this launch fails, it will be devastating and I'll never recover my credibility on this team → My psychological immune system will kick in; I'll adapt, find new projects, and this will feel much smaller in 18 months than it does right now.

models.name: Plan Continuation Bias
models.one_liner: We persist with an original plan even when new information clearly indicates we should change course.
models.problem: Causes aviation accidents, business failures, and military disasters when original plans no longer fit changed reality.
models.how_it_works: Desire for structure and goal commitment creates rigidity. The sunk cost of the original plan and the cognitive cost of re-planning both push toward continuation. Organizations codify this as 'stay the course' despite changing conditions. Pre-established decision gates and explicit 'what would cause us to change plan?' criteria counteract it.
models.origin: NTSB accident reports in aviation; Charles Dismukes and colleagues formalized the concept in safety research.
models.scenario: A startup's original go-to-market plan targeted enterprise clients, but three months in, all traction is coming from SMBs — the CEO keeps pushing enterprise sales because 'that was the plan.'
models.prompt: Ask: what specific signal would have to appear for you to officially declare the original plan wrong? Does that signal already exist in your current data?
models.before_after: We committed to enterprise, we've built the sales deck for enterprise, and we need to stay the course — → The data is showing a completely different customer profile than our original plan assumed; staying the course now is ignoring evidence, not showing discipline.

models.name: Google Effect (Digital Amnesia)
models.one_liner: We fail to encode information into memory when we know it's stored and easily retrievable externally.
models.problem: Outsourcing memory to search engines reduces internalized knowledge and the deep connections that enable creative thinking.
models.how_it_works: Sparrow et al. (2011) showed people remember where to find information better than the information itself when they believe it will be saved. The brain rationally offloads to external storage, but loses the serendipitous mental connections that come from holding knowledge internally. Related to the Extended Mind Thesis — but with costs to deep understanding.
models.origin: Betsy Sparrow, Jenny Liu, and Daniel Wegner, 2011.
models.scenario: A junior software engineer always looks up the same syntax and API patterns without ever internalizing them, and struggles to think through architecture problems in whiteboard interviews because the knowledge never sticks.
models.prompt: Identify one topic you look up repeatedly — spend 20 minutes today learning it without a browser open, then close your laptop and explain it out loud to yourself to force retrieval.
models.before_after: I can just Google that whenever I need it, so there's no point memorizing it → Internalizing knowledge creates deep mental connections that enable creative problem-solving, while pure lookup-dependence leaves me unable to reason about things I technically 'know'.

models.name: Pomodoro Technique
models.one_liner: Break work into 25-minute focused sprints separated by short breaks to maintain concentration.
models.problem: Mental fatigue and distraction erode focus over long uninterrupted work sessions.
models.how_it_works: Set a timer for 25 minutes, work with full focus on one task, take a 5-minute break. After 4 Pomodoros, take a 15-30 minute break. The timer creates artificial urgency (countering Parkinson's Law) and the breaks prevent cognitive depletion.
models.origin: Francesco Cirillo, late 1980s; named after a tomato-shaped kitchen timer (pomodoro = Italian for tomato)
models.scenario: A freelance writer is struggling to finish a feature article, spending three hours nominally working but constantly checking Twitter and Slack and producing only two paragraphs.
models.prompt: Set a physical timer for 25 minutes, close every tab except your document, and commit to writing — even badly — until the timer rings, then take a genuine 5-minute break before evaluating output.
models.before_after: I'll work on this article all afternoon and see how much I get done → I will work in four focused 25-minute blocks with real breaks between them, and I will produce more in two hours than I have all day.

models.name: Getting Things Done (GTD)
models.one_liner: Capture every commitment into a trusted external system to free the mind for actual thinking.
models.problem: Mental overhead of tracking open loops (tasks, commitments, ideas) drains cognitive capacity needed for deep work.
models.how_it_works: Five stages: Capture (externalize everything), Clarify (is it actionable?), Organize (by context/priority), Reflect (weekly review), Engage (do the right thing now). The key insight: your brain is for having ideas, not holding them.
models.origin: David Allen, book 'Getting Things Done', 2001
models.scenario: A marketing director feels constantly overwhelmed, mentally rehearsing a dozen half-remembered commitments during meetings, and keeps dropping balls on small but important tasks.
models.prompt: Spend 30 minutes doing a full brain dump — write every open commitment, project, and nagging thought onto paper — then for each item ask: is this actionable, and what is the very next physical action?
models.before_after: I need to keep all my commitments in my head so I don't forget anything important → Every open loop lives in a trusted external system, so my brain is completely free to focus on the one thing in front of me right now.

models.name: Time Blocking
models.one_liner: Assign every hour of the workday a specific task or category of work — give every minute a job.
models.problem: Reactive, interrupt-driven work prevents sustained progress on deep, high-value tasks.
models.how_it_works: Plan your day in advance by blocking calendar slots for specific work types (deep work, admin, meetings, creative). Treat blocks as appointments with yourself. Leave buffer blocks for overflow. Cal Newport calls it 'the most productive thing I do.'
models.origin: Popularized by Cal Newport in 'Deep Work' (2016); concept predates Newport but he systematized it
models.scenario: A software team lead spends their day reacting to Slack messages and calendar invites, ending each day feeling busy but unable to point to any significant work they completed on their own projects.
models.prompt: Before tomorrow begins, block out your calendar with named slots for each type of work — including a 'deep work' block with no notifications — and treat those blocks as non-negotiable as any external meeting.
models.before_after: I'll work on the architecture design whenever I find a free moment between meetings → I have a protected 9-11am block every morning for deep technical work, and meetings can only be scheduled after 11am.

models.name: Eat the Frog
models.one_liner: Tackle your most important (and dreaded) task first thing in the morning before anything else.
models.problem: Procrastination on high-impact tasks because easier, lower-value work gets done first.
models.how_it_works: Identify the single most important task — the one you're most likely to avoid. Do it immediately, before email or meetings. Decision fatigue and willpower are highest at day start. Completing it creates momentum for the rest of the day.
models.origin: Brian Tracy, book 'Eat That Frog!' (2001); attributed conceptually to a Mark Twain quote
models.scenario: A sales manager has to deliver difficult performance feedback to a top performer who has been causing team friction — she has been delaying it for two weeks, starting each day with emails and reports instead.
models.prompt: Identify the one task you have been most actively avoiding this week, schedule it as the first thing tomorrow morning before any email or Slack, and write down exactly what you will say or do to start it.
models.before_after: I'll clear my inbox first and then get to the difficult conversation when I have mental space → The difficult conversation IS the most important thing on my list; everything else is procrastination in disguise, and willpower is highest first thing in the morning.

models.name: Two-Minute Rule
models.one_liner: If a task takes less than two minutes, do it immediately rather than capturing it in a system.
models.problem: Small tasks accumulate in to-do lists and create overhead disproportionate to their actual execution time.
models.how_it_works: When processing inputs (email, requests, ideas), apply the test: can this be done in under 2 minutes? If yes, do it now. If no, defer, delegate, or delete. The overhead of tracking often exceeds the task itself for micro-tasks.
models.origin: David Allen, 'Getting Things Done' (2001); a core GTD heuristic
models.scenario: A team lead has 47 items on their to-do list, many of which are simple replies or five-second approvals that have been sitting there for days creating guilt and visual clutter.
models.prompt: Go through your task list and flag every item that takes under two minutes to complete — handle all of them right now, sequentially, without capturing them anywhere.
models.before_after: I'll add that quick email response to my to-do list and get to it later → This reply will take 90 seconds; doing it now costs less than the overhead of capturing, revisiting, and eventually doing it later.

models.name: Batching
models.one_liner: Group similar tasks together and process them in a single dedicated block to reduce context-switching cost.
models.problem: Constant context-switching between different task types (email, creative work, calls) destroys flow and wastes cognitive ramp-up time.
models.how_it_works: Cluster similar tasks — all email in one block, all calls in another, all writing in another. Each switch between task types costs 10-20 minutes of reorientation time. Batching amortizes that cost. Works best combined with Time Blocking.
models.origin: Operations management principle (batch processing); popularized in knowledge work by Tim Ferriss in 'The 4-Hour Workweek' (2007)
models.scenario: A product manager answers Slack messages as they arrive all day, interrupting design reviews, writing sessions, and strategic planning every 8-12 minutes throughout the workday.
models.prompt: Map your current interruption points across a typical workday, then redesign it by grouping all communication responses into two 30-minute windows and protecting all other blocks from context switching.
models.before_after: I respond to messages as they come in so nothing falls through the cracks → I process all messages at 10am and 4pm; between those windows I am unreachable, and I produce in two focused hours what previously took six fragmented ones.

models.name: Deep Work
models.one_liner: Professional activities performed in distraction-free concentration that push cognitive limits produce rare and valuable output.
models.problem: Knowledge work is increasingly fragmented by shallow tasks (email, meetings, social media), producing busyness without real output.
models.how_it_works: Schedule protected blocks (2-4+ hours) of single-task focus with zero interruptions. Eliminate shallow work ruthlessly. Cultivate the ability to concentrate deeply as a skill — it atrophies without practice. Use rituals to enter and exit deep work states.
models.origin: Cal Newport, book 'Deep Work: Rules for Focused Success in a Distracted World' (2016)
models.scenario: A machine learning researcher needs to work through a complex model architecture problem but her calendar is packed with standups, one-on-ones, and async reviews that fragment every day into 45-minute chunks.
models.prompt: Audit your calendar for next week and identify two 3-hour blocks you could protect from all meetings and notifications — then design a shutdown ritual that signals the end of each deep work session.
models.before_after: I'll squeeze in work on the architecture problem between meetings throughout the day → I will block Tuesday and Thursday mornings as sacred deep work time; the fragmented slots between meetings are not sufficient for the cognitive depth this problem requires.

models.name: Flow State
models.one_liner: Optimal performance occurs in a state of full immersion where challenge matches skill — time disappears and output peaks.
models.problem: Work that is too easy breeds boredom; too hard breeds anxiety. Neither produces peak performance.
models.how_it_works: Flow is triggered when task difficulty slightly exceeds current skill level, creating engaged stretch. Prerequisites: clear goals, immediate feedback, perceived control, minimal interruption. To induce flow: remove distractions, set clear micro-goals, match task to skill level.
models.origin: Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi, psychologist; coined the concept in research in the 1970s, popularized in book 'Flow' (1990)
models.scenario: A game developer feels bored and unchallenged when doing routine bug fixes but overwhelmed and anxious when tackling core engine architecture, and rarely feels genuinely productive.
models.prompt: Rate your current task on two axes: your skill level (1-10) and the task's difficulty (1-10) — if they differ by more than 2 points, adjust the task scope or skill application until they're within range.
models.before_after: I just need to push through this work whether it feels engaging or not → This task feels boring because it's far below my skill level; I can add a self-imposed constraint to make it a genuine stretch and trigger the engagement I need to do my best work.

models.name: SMART Goals
models.one_liner: Goals must be Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound to drive real execution.
models.problem: Vague goals ('be more productive', 'grow the business') provide no clear path to action or completion criteria.
models.how_it_works: For each goal, test against 5 criteria: Specific (what exactly?), Measurable (how will you know?), Achievable (realistic stretch?), Relevant (aligned with larger purpose?), Time-bound (by when?). A goal failing any criterion should be rewritten before acting.
models.origin: First formalized by George T. Doran in a 1981 Management Review paper 'There's a SMART way to write management's goals and objectives'
models.scenario: A startup founder sets a goal to 'grow revenue this quarter' and then struggles to know whether to focus on new customer acquisition, reducing churn, or upselling existing accounts.
models.prompt: Take your current top goal and rewrite it by filling in: by [date], we will achieve [specific number] of [measurable outcome], verified by [data source], because it is [realistic given current trajectory] and advances [strategic priority].
models.before_after: Our goal is to grow the business this quarter → By September 30, we will sign 12 new enterprise contracts worth at least $8k ARR each, tracked in Salesforce, representing a 40% increase over last quarter.

models.name: OKRs (Objectives and Key Results)
models.one_liner: Connect ambitious qualitative objectives to 3-5 specific measurable outcomes that define what achievement looks like.
models.problem: Goals either stay abstract and uninspiring or get too tactical without connecting to strategic direction.
models.how_it_works: Set an Objective (inspiring, qualitative, what you want to achieve) plus 3-5 Key Results (specific, measurable outcomes that would prove the objective was met). Review quarterly. Grade 0-1 — a 0.7 grade is ideal; 1.0 means the goal wasn't ambitious enough.
models.origin: Andy Grove at Intel in the 1970s; evangelized at Google by John Doerr (1999); popularized by John Doerr's book 'Measure What Matters' (2018)
models.scenario: A 30-person SaaS company has annual goals but teams are working on disconnected initiatives with no shared sense of what winning looks like for the quarter.
models.prompt: Write one Objective that would make your team genuinely excited if achieved, then ask: what are the three things that would absolutely have to be measurably true for an outside observer to confirm we achieved it?
models.before_after: Our goal this quarter is to improve the product and grow the user base → Our Objective is to become the go-to tool for enterprise onboarding teams, measured by: 20 new enterprise logos, NPS above 50 from onboarding users, and 80% 90-day retention.

models.name: North Star Metric
models.one_liner: One single metric that best captures the core value your product delivers — everything else aligns to move this number.
models.problem: Teams optimize local metrics (revenue, downloads, page views) that don't reflect whether the product is genuinely delivering value.
models.how_it_works: Identify the single metric that correlates most strongly with long-term customer value and retention. All teams should understand how their work affects this metric. It guides prioritization but must be paired with guardrail metrics to prevent gaming.
models.origin: Product management practice popularized at companies like Airbnb (nights booked), Facebook (DAU), Spotify (time listening); codified by Sean Ellis and growth hacking community circa 2010s
models.scenario: A consumer app team is celebrating record downloads while churn is accelerating and revenue is flat, and different teams are optimizing for completely different metrics.
models.prompt: Ask: which single number, if it doubled, would make us certain the business is healthy and customers are getting real value — and does your current roadmap actually move that number?
models.before_after: We're tracking downloads, DAU, revenue, session length, and NPS — all looking good in different ways → Downloads are a vanity metric; our north star is weekly active users who complete a core workflow, because that is what actually predicts retention and revenue.

models.name: Porter's Five Forces
models.one_liner: Map five structural forces — rivalry, buyer power, supplier power, substitutes, new entrants — to assess industry profitability.
models.problem: Companies set strategy based on direct competitor moves alone, missing the structural forces that actually determine long-run profitability.
models.how_it_works: Score each of five forces (competitive rivalry, threat of new entrants, threat of substitutes, buyer bargaining power, supplier bargaining power) as high/medium/low. High forces compress margins. Strategy aims to find positions where forces are weak, or to change the forces themselves.
models.origin: Michael E. Porter, Harvard Business School, book 'Competitive Strategy' (1980)
models.scenario: A founder is considering entering the project management software market, excited by its large TAM but not thinking beyond how to differentiate from Asana and Monday.com.
models.prompt: Score each of the five forces (rivalry, new entrant threat, substitute threat, buyer power, supplier power) as high/medium/low, then ask: given this structure, where is the margin actually going to go — to customers, to suppliers, or to us?
models.before_after: If we can out-execute Asana on UX, we can win a slice of this huge market → Even with a better product, this industry has intense rivalry, near-zero switching costs, and free substitutes — the structural forces are working against sustainable margins regardless of feature quality.

models.name: SWOT Analysis
models.one_liner: Map internal Strengths and Weaknesses against external Opportunities and Threats to guide strategic choices.
models.problem: Strategy is formed without systematically auditing what the organization can and can't do relative to what the environment offers.
models.how_it_works: Fill a 2x2: internal (Strengths, Weaknesses) and external (Opportunities, Threats). Then cross-analyze: use strengths to exploit opportunities (SO), use strengths to avoid threats (ST), address weaknesses to capture opportunities (WO), minimize weakness + threat exposure (WT).
models.origin: Developed at Stanford Research Institute by Albert Humphrey et al. in the 1960s-70s through Fortune 500 research
models.scenario: A regional restaurant chain is deciding whether to expand nationally, and the CEO is excited about the opportunity without systematically accounting for what could go wrong.
models.prompt: Fill a 2x2 with your actual internal realities (Strengths, Weaknesses) and external environment (Opportunities, Threats), then cross-analyze: does your most dangerous weakness directly overlap with your most serious threat?
models.before_after: We have a strong brand and there is clear demand in new markets, so we should expand → Our supply chain is our biggest weakness, and labor cost inflation is our biggest threat — those two things collide in a national expansion in a way that could destroy the margins that make this business work.

models.name: Blue Ocean Strategy
models.one_liner: Create uncontested market space by making competition irrelevant through value innovation — offer more value at lower cost.
models.problem: Most companies fight in 'red oceans' (existing competitive markets), competing on the same dimensions and eroding margins.
models.how_it_works: Use the Four Actions Framework: Eliminate factors the industry takes for granted, Reduce factors below industry standard, Raise factors above industry standard, Create new factors the industry never offered. The result: a new value curve that serves non-customers and defies the cost-differentiation tradeoff.
models.origin: W. Chan Kim and Renée Mauborgne, INSEAD; book 'Blue Ocean Strategy' (2005)
models.scenario: A fitness studio owner is struggling to compete on price and features against large gym chains and boutique competitors who all offer similar class formats and equipment.
models.prompt: Apply the Four Actions Framework: list what your industry treats as essential that you could eliminate entirely, what you could raise far above industry standard, and what new value element has never been offered in this space.
models.before_after: We need to add more equipment, lower prices, and run more class types to compete → Instead of fighting on the same dimensions as every other gym, we could eliminate the equipment floor entirely, raise personalized programming far above the standard, and create a social accountability layer that no gym currently offers.

models.name: BCG Matrix (Growth-Share Matrix)
models.one_liner: Plot business units or products on market growth vs. relative market share to guide portfolio investment decisions.
models.problem: Resource allocation across multiple products or business units is made subjectively without a systematic framework.
models.how_it_works: Place each product in one of four cells: Stars (high growth, high share — invest), Cash Cows (low growth, high share — harvest for cash), Question Marks (high growth, low share — decide: invest or divest), Dogs (low growth, low share — divest or minimize). Cash cows fund stars.
models.origin: Bruce D. Henderson, Boston Consulting Group, 1970
models.scenario: A CPG company has six product lines of varying age and performance, and the CFO is spreading R&D and marketing budget roughly equally across all of them to 'be fair.'
models.prompt: Plot each product on a 2x2 of market growth rate vs. your relative market share, label each quadrant (Star/Cash Cow/Question Mark/Dog), then ask: are your cash cows actually funding your stars, or are your dogs consuming resources your stars urgently need?
models.before_after: We invest equally across product lines because every brand deserves a fair chance → Two of our products are Cash Cows generating steady margin — that cash should be pouring into our one Star, not propping up three Dogs with no growth path.

models.name: Jobs to Be Done (JTBD)
models.one_liner: Customers don't buy products — they hire them to get a specific job done in their lives.
models.problem: Product decisions based on customer demographics or competitor features miss the actual motivation behind purchase and use.
models.how_it_works: For each product/feature, define the functional, social, and emotional 'job' the customer is trying to accomplish. Identify the struggling moment that prompts hiring a solution. Compete at the job level, not the product category level. Reveals non-obvious competitors and unserved needs.
models.origin: Tony Ulwick coined JTBD in 1990; Clayton Christensen popularized it in 'The Innovator's Solution' (2003) and Harvard Business Review
models.scenario: A B2B software product team is losing deals to a spreadsheet and can't understand why, even though their feature set is objectively more powerful than any competitor.
models.prompt: Interview three recent churned customers or lost deals and ask: what were you doing before our product, what prompted you to look for something different, and what job were you actually trying to get done when you chose a solution?
models.before_after: We keep losing to spreadsheets because they're cheaper, so we need to lower our price → Customers aren't hiring us to manage data — they're hiring a solution to avoid the anxiety of looking unprepared in front of their VP; a spreadsheet gives them control and familiarity, and we haven't addressed that emotional job at all.

models.name: Lean Canvas
