models.one_liner: A one-page business model focused on problems, solutions, metrics, and unfair advantage — built for early-stage validation.
models.problem: Traditional business plans take months and become obsolete before execution; founders need a rapid model for testing assumptions.
models.how_it_works: Fill 9 boxes: Problem (top 3 problems), Solution, Unique Value Proposition, Unfair Advantage, Customer Segments, Key Metrics, Channels, Cost Structure, Revenue Streams. Start with the problem box, not the solution. Each box is a hypothesis to test, not a commitment.
models.origin: Ash Maurya, 2010; adaptation of Alexander Osterwalder's Business Model Canvas, layered with Lean Startup principles
models.scenario: A first-time founder has spent three months drafting a 40-page business plan for a B2B SaaS tool before ever talking to a single customer.
models.prompt: Fill the 9 boxes of your Lean Canvas in under 60 minutes — starting with the Problem box, not the Solution. For each box, write one sentence that describes your assumption and one sentence describing how you will test it this week.
models.before_after: The founder polishes financials in a spreadsheet assuming customers want what he built → The founder identifies his riskiest assumption (that HR managers hate manual onboarding) and books 5 customer calls before writing a single line of code.

models.name: Minimum Viable Product (MVP)
models.one_liner: The smallest version of a product that collects maximum validated learning about customers with minimum effort.
models.problem: Teams build full products based on unvalidated assumptions, wasting months of effort on features nobody wants.
models.how_it_works: Identify the riskiest assumption in the business model. Build the simplest possible artifact (landing page, prototype, concierge service) that tests that assumption with real customers. Measure behavior (not opinions). Learn. Decide: persist, pivot, or kill. Speed of learning beats thoroughness of build.
models.origin: Frank Robinson coined 'MVP' circa 2001; Eric Ries systematized it in 'The Lean Startup' (2011)
models.scenario: A product team is planning a 6-month build for an AI-powered recipe app with 50 features, based entirely on internal assumptions about what users want.
models.prompt: Write down the single riskiest assumption your product is built on. Then design the smallest possible test — a landing page, a manual process, a prototype — that would tell you within 2 weeks whether that assumption is true or false.
models.before_after: The team builds all 50 features over 6 months and launches to near-zero engagement → The team puts up a landing page with a waitlist and discovers users want meal planning, not just recipes, before any code is written.

models.name: Build-Measure-Learn
models.one_liner: The core Lean Startup feedback loop: build something, measure what happens, learn whether to continue or pivot.
models.problem: Long development cycles with no customer feedback produce products disconnected from market reality.
models.how_it_works: Start with a hypothesis (leap of faith assumption). Build the smallest possible test. Measure using actionable metrics (not vanity metrics). Learn: was the hypothesis valid? If yes, persist and optimize. If no, pivot — make a structured change to strategy. Minimize time per loop.
models.origin: Eric Ries, 'The Lean Startup' (2011); adapted from the Deming/Shewhart PDCA cycle for startup contexts
models.scenario: A startup has been in 'stealth mode' for 8 months building a marketplace, planning to launch with a complete product to make a big splash.
models.prompt: Map your current riskiest hypothesis onto the loop: What will you Build (smallest test artifact)? What will you Measure (one specific behavior metric, not a survey)? What will you Learn (what specific outcome confirms or kills the hypothesis)? Set a two-week deadline.
models.before_after: The team builds for 8 months in isolation and launches to silence because the demand assumption was wrong → The team runs a concierge MVP in week 3, measures repeat usage, and pivots supply-side strategy before investing in platform infrastructure.

models.name: Flywheel Effect
models.one_liner: Sustained, focused effort in one direction compounds over time — momentum builds until the flywheel spins itself.
models.problem: Organizations seek single breakthrough moments or silver bullets, missing that greatness comes from compound incremental improvements.
models.how_it_works: Map a virtuous cycle specific to the business (e.g., Amazon: low prices → more customers → more sellers → more selection → lower costs → lower prices). Push consistently on every component. No single push creates the momentum — it's the accumulation. Identify and reinforce your unique flywheel.
models.origin: Jim Collins, 'Good to Great' (2001); Amazon famously applied it as a core strategic framework
models.scenario: A SaaS company is chasing quarterly growth by constantly launching new products and pivoting marketing messages, but growth stalls after each campaign spike.
models.prompt: Draw your company's specific virtuous cycle — the 5-7 components that, if each one strengthens the next, would create self-sustaining momentum. Identify which single component in your cycle is currently weakest, and commit all Q3 investment there.
models.before_after: Leadership launches a new product category every quarter hoping one will be the breakthrough → Leadership maps their flywheel, identifies that retention is weakest, doubles down on customer success, and watch organic referrals compound over 18 months.

models.name: Compounding
models.one_liner: Small consistent gains multiply exponentially over time — the most powerful force in business, investing, and learning.
models.problem: Humans undervalue gradual improvement because the exponential nature of compounding is counterintuitive to linear thinking.
models.how_it_works: Apply the compound growth formula: a 1% daily improvement yields 37x improvement over a year. Identify compounding assets: skills, reputation, relationships, code, content, capital. Protect them. Avoid interruptions — compounding is destroyed by volatility. Time in the game beats peak performance.
models.origin: Mathematical concept; applied to business by Warren Buffett and Charlie Munger; popularized as a mental model by Naval Ravikant and James Clear
models.scenario: A junior software engineer feels discouraged because she cannot see meaningful progress despite consistently learning and practicing for 6 months.
models.prompt: Identify one compounding asset you are building (a skill, a relationship network, a code library, a content archive). Calculate what 1% daily improvement looks like at 1 year (37x) and 5 years. Now ask: what is the one habit that, if maintained without interruption, would compound most powerfully in your domain?
models.before_after: The engineer switches focus every few weeks chasing whatever skill is trending, accumulating shallow knowledge across many areas → The engineer commits to deep daily practice in distributed systems, compounds expertise over two years, and becomes the team's go-to architect.

models.name: Theory of Constraints (TOC)
models.one_liner: Every system has exactly one bottleneck limiting output — improve nothing else until you fix that constraint.
models.problem: Optimization efforts spread across the whole system produce minimal gains; most work is on non-bottlenecks.
models.how_it_works: Five Focusing Steps: (1) Identify the constraint. (2) Exploit it — squeeze maximum throughput without changing anything. (3) Subordinate everything else to the constraint. (4) Elevate the constraint — invest to increase its capacity. (5) Repeat — once fixed, a new constraint emerges. Never optimize non-constraints.
models.origin: Eliyahu M. Goldratt, Israeli physicist/businessman, book 'The Goal' (1984)
models.scenario: A manufacturing plant is running three simultaneous efficiency initiatives — on the warehouse, the assembly line, and the shipping dock — but overall output has not improved in six months.
models.prompt: Walk the five focusing steps: (1) Where is your current constraint — the single step where work piles up longest? (2) What can you do TODAY to squeeze more throughput from it without spending money? (3) What are you currently doing upstream or downstream that is irrelevant until the constraint is fixed? Stop those activities now.
models.before_after: Managers optimize shipping dock speed while the assembly line bottleneck creates growing queues that cancel out every gain → The plant identifies assembly as the constraint, reassigns two warehouse staff to support it, and throughput increases 22% in six weeks without any capital investment.

models.name: Bottleneck Analysis
models.one_liner: Systematically identify which single resource or step constrains total system throughput.
models.problem: Performance improvement efforts are applied uniformly across a process when only one constraint actually limits output.
models.how_it_works: Map the full value stream or process. Find the step with the lowest capacity or longest queue. That step is the bottleneck — it determines the system's output ceiling. Remove or expand it, then find the next constraint. Use data (cycle times, queue depths) not intuition.
models.origin: Industrial engineering; formalized in Theory of Constraints by Eliyahu Goldratt (1984)
models.scenario: A software development team is missing every sprint deadline, and the engineering manager is pressuring all developers to work faster across the board.
models.prompt: Map each stage of your delivery process (backlog refinement, design, coding, code review, QA, deployment) and measure the average queue length and wait time at each stage this week. Circle the stage with the longest queue — that is your bottleneck. What is one change you can make to that stage only?
models.before_after: The manager pushes the whole team to move faster, creating busywork and burnout without improving delivery dates → The manager measures and discovers code review is the bottleneck with a 4-day average wait, adds a second senior reviewer, and cycle time drops by 40%.

models.name: Critical Path Method (CPM)
models.one_liner: Identify the longest chain of dependent tasks in a project — that chain determines the minimum completion time.
models.problem: Projects slip because resources are applied to non-critical tasks while the true duration bottleneck (the critical path) gets insufficient attention.
models.how_it_works: Map all tasks, their durations, and dependencies. Calculate earliest/latest start-finish for each. The critical path is the longest chain with zero float — any delay here delays the whole project. Compress the project by accelerating critical path tasks only.
models.origin: James E. Kelley Jr. (Remington Rand) and Morgan R. Walker (DuPont), late 1950s
models.scenario: A product launch is two weeks behind schedule and the project manager has asked every team member to work overtime to catch up.
models.prompt: Draw every remaining task as a node. Connect tasks with arrows showing dependencies. Calculate the longest chain of dependent tasks from now to launch — that is your critical path. Which tasks on that path have zero slack? Assign your best people exclusively to those tasks, and deprioritize everything else.
models.before_after: The PM calls an all-hands crunch that has everyone scrambling on tasks that will not affect the launch date → The PM identifies that legal review and API integration are on the critical path while the marketing website is not, redirects resources, and recovers a full week.

models.name: PDCA Cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act)
models.one_liner: Iterative four-step cycle for continuous improvement: plan a change, do it, check results, act on what you learned.
models.problem: Organizations implement changes without structured feedback loops, repeating mistakes or failing to lock in improvements.
models.how_it_works: Plan: identify a problem and hypothesize a solution. Do: run a small experiment. Check: measure outcomes against the hypothesis. Act: if it worked, standardize; if not, revise the hypothesis. Loop indefinitely. Each cycle produces learning, not just output.
models.origin: Walter Shewhart (1930s, Bell Labs) developed the foundation; W. Edwards Deming taught and popularized it in postwar Japan
models.scenario: A hospital unit is experiencing recurring medication errors and has attempted several fixes — new checklists, staff briefings — that have not reduced the error rate.
models.prompt: Run one PDCA cycle this week: Plan — what is your single most specific hypothesis about why errors occur? Do — implement one small, controlled change with that hypothesis. Check — measure the error rate over 5 days. Act — did the data support the hypothesis? If yes, standardize. If no, update the hypothesis before the next cycle.
models.before_after: The unit tries multiple simultaneous interventions and cannot tell which if any are working → The unit tests one hypothesis at a time (hand-off timing is the cause), confirms it with data, standardizes the fix, and errors drop 60% within a month.

models.name: Wardley Mapping
models.one_liner: Map every component of your value chain by visibility to the user and evolutionary maturity — then exploit where things are heading.
models.problem: Strategy is made without situational awareness — leaders don't know what's evolving into a commodity or where competitors will attack next.
models.how_it_works: Draw a map: Y-axis = visibility to user (user need at top, infrastructure at bottom). X-axis = evolution (genesis → custom → product → commodity). Position every component. Patterns emerge: where to build vs. buy, where disruption will come, where to invest. Anticipate moves because evolution is predictable.
models.origin: Simon Wardley, Fotango, 2005; developed further at Canonical UK 2008-2010
models.scenario: A CTO is deciding whether to build a proprietary infrastructure logging system or buy a commercial solution, and the engineering team is excited to build it in-house.
models.prompt: Place your logging system on a Wardley Map X-axis: is it in Genesis (novel), Custom-Built, Product, or Commodity? If it is at Product or Commodity stage in your industry, buying is almost always correct. Ask: what activities in our stack are still in Genesis where custom-building creates differentiation?
models.before_after: The CTO approves a 3-month build of a logging system because the team wants ownership, while competitors ship features instead → The CTO maps logging as a commodity, buys a $200/month SaaS tool, and redirects engineers to the novel recommendation engine where custom-building creates real advantage.

models.name: Ansoff Matrix
models.one_liner: Four growth strategies mapped by product (existing/new) and market (existing/new) to assess risk and direction.
models.problem: Growth strategy defaults to one approach without systematically evaluating the risk/reward of different expansion directions.
models.how_it_works: Four cells: Market Penetration (existing product, existing market — lowest risk), Market Development (existing product, new market), Product Development (new product, existing market), Diversification (new product, new market — highest risk). Risk increases as you move from known to unknown dimensions.
models.origin: H. Igor Ansoff, 'Strategies for Diversification', Harvard Business Review, 1957
models.scenario: A consumer packaged goods brand is considering three growth options simultaneously: a price promotion, a new product line, entering Asian markets, and acquiring a competitor.
models.prompt: Place each growth option in the Ansoff Matrix 2x2 (existing vs. new product, existing vs. new market). Risk increases sharply as you move away from existing dimensions. For each option in a new dimension, ask: do we have evidence that our capabilities transfer, and can we afford to be wrong?
models.before_after: The leadership team debates all four options equally without weighing their very different risk profiles → The team recognizes the acquisition (diversification) carries 4x the risk of a price promotion (market penetration), sequences the low-risk moves first, and builds capital before entering new markets.

models.name: McKinsey 7S Framework
models.one_liner: Seven interdependent organizational elements must be aligned for strategy execution — change one, all others must adapt.
models.problem: Strategic plans fail in implementation because hard factors (structure, strategy, systems) are changed without adjusting soft factors (skills, staff, style, shared values).
models.how_it_works: Map the seven elements: Strategy, Structure, Systems (hard/tangible) and Shared Values, Skills, Staff, Style (soft/cultural). The center — Shared Values — is the most important. Any organizational change must identify ripple effects across all seven. Misalignment diagnoses execution failure.
models.origin: Tom Peters and Robert Waterman Jr., McKinsey & Company, 1980; published in 'In Search of Excellence' (1982)
models.scenario: A retail company is implementing a digital transformation strategy: new software, restructured teams, and a customer-first mandate — but 9 months in, adoption is near zero.
models.prompt: Map all 7 elements (Strategy, Structure, Systems, Shared Values, Skills, Staff, Style) for both your current state and your desired future state. For each element that has NOT changed to match the new strategy, ask: is this misalignment the silent killer of adoption? Prioritize fixing the gap in Shared Values first.
models.before_after: Leadership assumes the new CRM software will drive the cultural shift and blames employees for slow adoption → Leadership realizes that management style (still rewarding individual sales over customer relationships) directly contradicts the customer-first strategy, and changes the incentive system first.

models.name: PESTLE Analysis
models.one_liner: Scan six macro-environmental forces — Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, Environmental — to surface external opportunities and threats.
models.problem: Organizations develop strategy in an internal vacuum, missing macro shifts that will reshape the competitive landscape.
models.how_it_works: For each of 6 dimensions, identify current state and trajectory. Rate impact and likelihood. Use findings to stress-test existing strategy and identify emerging opportunities. Typically feeds into SWOT (PESTLE findings become Opportunities and Threats). Best done as a team with diverse perspectives.
models.origin: Attributed to Francis Aguilar's 'ETPS' scan in 'Scanning the Business Environment' (1967); extended to PEST, then PESTLE over decades
models.scenario: A fintech startup is building a lending product for gig workers and is about to close a Series A, with no structured view of the macro environment they are entering.
models.prompt: Run through all 6 PESTLE dimensions in 90 minutes with your founding team. For each dimension, write down: (1) the one trend that could most help your business in 3 years, and (2) the one trend that could kill it. Feed the top 3 threats directly into your risk register before the Series A closes.
models.before_after: The founding team focuses entirely on product and growth, blind to the regulatory wave that will restrict alternative lending within 18 months → The team identifies the Legal dimension as highest risk, hires a compliance advisor pre-Series A, and builds regulatory approval into their roadmap.

models.name: Value Chain Analysis
models.one_liner: Map every activity that creates customer value and identify where competitive advantage is actually built or lost.
models.problem: Cost and differentiation advantages are assumed to live in obvious places (price, product) while the real source is in specific operational activities.
models.how_it_works: Separate activities into Primary (inbound logistics, operations, outbound logistics, marketing/sales, service) and Support (procurement, technology, HR, infrastructure). For each, assess cost and differentiation contribution. Competitive advantage concentrates in a few activities — find them, strengthen them, and align the whole chain.
models.origin: Michael E. Porter, 'Competitive Advantage: Creating and Sustaining Superior Performance' (1985)
models.scenario: A premium coffee roaster is losing margin despite strong brand recognition and premium pricing, and the CEO is considering a price increase to fix profitability.
models.prompt: List every primary and support activity in your value chain. For each activity, estimate its cost and ask: does this activity create differentiation that customers pay for, or is it a cost center? Find the two activities where your competitive advantage actually lives — and ask whether you are under-investing in them while over-investing in commoditized steps.
models.before_after: The CEO raises prices without understanding where margin is leaking, losing wholesale accounts → The CEO discovers that outbound logistics and third-party distribution are commoditized cost centers eating 18% of margin, negotiates direct retail deals, and restores profitability without a price increase.

models.name: Theory of Constraints — Drum-Buffer-Rope
models.one_liner: Schedule production to the rhythm of the bottleneck (drum), protect it with inventory buffers, and rope the release of work to its pace.
models.problem: Traditional production scheduling overloads the system, creates variability, and hides the bottleneck.
models.how_it_works: Drum: let the bottleneck set the production pace. Buffer: place inventory protection before the bottleneck to prevent starvation. Rope: release raw materials only fast enough to feed the bottleneck. This eliminates WIP explosion while maximizing throughput. A specialized TOC scheduling technique.
models.origin: Eliyahu M. Goldratt, 'The Goal' (1984) and subsequent TOC Institute teachings
models.scenario: A custom furniture manufacturer is experiencing constant chaos — some orders rush through while others sit untouched for weeks, and WIP inventory is clogging the factory floor.
models.prompt: Identify your drum (the bottleneck workstation that sets pace). Place a physical buffer of 2-3 days' worth of inventory before that station. Then rope new raw material release to the drum's pace — only release new jobs when the buffer drops below a threshold. Measure WIP on the floor after 30 days.
models.before_after: Schedulers release new jobs daily to keep all workers busy, creating mountains of WIP and hiding the real bottleneck → The factory implements DBR, WIP drops 60%, the bottleneck workstation runs at full capacity, and on-time delivery improves from 62% to 89% in one quarter.

models.name: Hedgehog Concept
models.one_liner: Great organizations find and focus on the intersection of what they're passionate about, what they can be best at, and what drives their economic engine.
models.problem: Companies pursue growth in too many directions, diluting focus and preventing the sustained momentum needed to go from good to great.
models.how_it_works: Identify the overlap of three circles: (1) What you are deeply passionate about, (2) What you can be the best in the world at, (3) What best drives your economic engine. Everything outside that intersection is a distraction. This is not an aspiration — it's a discovery about current reality.
models.origin: Jim Collins, 'Good to Great' (2001); inspired by Isaiah Berlin's essay on the Greek parable of the hedgehog vs. the fox
models.scenario: A 7-year-old digital agency is constantly chasing new service offerings — adding video production, social media management, and PR — because every new client requests something different.
models.prompt: Draw three overlapping circles. In circle 1, write what your team is genuinely passionate about. In circle 2, write what you can realistically be the best in your region or market at. In circle 3, write what your economic engine actually runs on. Only the intersection of all three circles is your Hedgehog. List every current service offering that falls outside that intersection.
models.before_after: The agency says yes to every new service request, spreading talent thin and losing to specialists on every pitch → The agency discovers their hedgehog is B2B content strategy, drops video and PR, becomes the clear leader in their niche, and doubles revenue per employee in two years.

models.name: Kanban
models.one_liner: Visualize workflow on a board, limit work-in-progress, and pull work through stages only when capacity exists.
models.problem: Invisible work queues, multitasking, and overloaded systems slow throughput and hide bottlenecks.
models.how_it_works: Create a board with columns (To Do → In Progress → Done). Set explicit WIP limits per column. New work enters only when capacity is available (pull system). Visualizing flow exposes bottlenecks and queue buildups immediately. Continuous flow beats batch processing.
models.origin: Taiichi Ohno, Toyota Production System, late 1950s; adapted for knowledge work by David J. Anderson circa 2007
models.scenario: A content team of 4 is managing 25 simultaneous article drafts across email, Slack, and a shared Google Drive, and nothing ever seems to get published on time.
models.prompt: Create a 4-column Kanban board (Backlog, Writing, Review, Published). Move every current piece of work onto the board. Set a WIP limit of 2 for the Writing column and 1 for Review. Enforce the rule: nothing new enters Writing until something exits it. Measure how many articles you publish in the next two weeks vs. the previous two.
models.before_after: Everyone on the team is working on 6 articles simultaneously, none reaching the finish line, and deadlines keep slipping → The team enforces WIP limits, finishes one article before starting another, and publication rate doubles while total stress decreases.

models.name: Agile / Scrum
models.one_liner: Deliver work in short iterative sprints with constant feedback loops rather than long sequential plans.
models.problem: Long development cycles without feedback produce outputs misaligned with actual needs; plans become obsolete faster than execution.
models.how_it_works: Work in 1-4 week sprints with defined goals. Daily standups surface blockers. Sprint reviews show work to stakeholders for feedback. Retrospectives improve the process itself. Product backlog is continuously reprioritized. Embraces change over following a fixed plan.
models.origin: Ken Schwaber and Jeff Sutherland, early 1990s; formalized in the 'Agile Manifesto' (2001)
models.scenario: A government IT department is 18 months into a 3-year waterfall project to replace a legacy benefits system, with the first user demo not planned until month 30.
models.prompt: Identify the highest-value slice of your project that could be made functional and shown to real users within 3 weeks. Define that as Sprint 1. Plan a demo with actual end users at the end of that sprint. List the three biggest assumptions about user needs that will be validated or invalidated by their feedback.
models.before_after: The project team builds detailed requirements for 18 months and discovers at the first demo that the user workflow they designed does not match how caseworkers actually work → The team gets user feedback after 3 weeks of work, adjusts the workflow assumptions early, and saves an estimated 6 months of rework.

models.name: PDSA Cycle (Plan-Do-Study-Act)
models.one_liner: Deming's preferred variant of PDCA — substitutes 'Study' for 'Check' to emphasize deep learning over mere verification.
models.problem: PDCA's 'Check' step is often treated as pass/fail verification rather than genuine learning about why something worked or didn't.
models.how_it_works: Same four steps as PDCA but 'Study' means deeply analyzing results, updating your theory of the system, and extracting causal understanding — not just confirming a metric hit or missed. This produces richer learning and better next-iteration hypotheses.
models.origin: W. Edwards Deming; he preferred PDSA and attributed the check/study distinction as important for scientific rigor
models.scenario: A primary care clinic has tried three different patient reminder systems to reduce no-shows, and each one seems to work for a month before the no-show rate climbs back up.
models.prompt: After your next small intervention, replace your Check step with deep Study: ask WHY the change worked or didn't work, not just whether it hit the target. Interview 5 no-show patients. Update your theory of the system. What does this tell you about the underlying cause — and how does that change your next hypothesis?
models.before_after: The clinic confirms that SMS reminders reduced no-shows last month and standardizes them, only to watch no-shows climb again because the root cause (transportation barriers) was never identified → The clinic studies why reminders failed for a subgroup, discovers the transportation issue, adds a ride-share partnership, and achieves a durable 35% reduction.

models.name: Unit Economics
models.one_liner: Analyze revenue and costs at the single-customer level — profitability per unit determines whether growth creates or destroys value.
models.problem: Growing companies can appear healthy at an aggregate level while losing money on every customer — scale amplifies a broken unit model.
models.how_it_works: Key metrics: LTV (lifetime gross profit per customer), CAC (cost to acquire one customer), LTV:CAC ratio (healthy = 3:1+), Payback Period (months to recover CAC). If LTV < CAC, growth destroys value. Fix unit economics before scaling. Each pricing, retention, or acquisition lever shifts the ratio.
models.origin: Venture capital and startup practice; formalized in the 2010s growth hacking and SaaS communities
models.scenario: A DTC e-commerce brand is celebrating 200% year-over-year revenue growth and is about to raise a $5M round to pour into paid acquisition, but the founder has never calculated LTV or CAC.
models.prompt: Before touching the raise, calculate these three numbers for your last cohort of customers: CAC (total sales and marketing spend divided by new customers acquired), LTV (average order value × purchase frequency × gross margin × average customer lifespan), and Payback Period (CAC divided by monthly gross profit per customer). If LTV:CAC is below 3:1, you are scaling a broken model.
models.before_after: The founder sees revenue growing 200% and assumes the business is healthy, raises $5M, and spends it on acquisition that loses money on every customer at scale → The founder calculates a 1.2:1 LTV:CAC ratio, pauses paid acquisition, fixes retention and gross margin first, and raises the round after reaching 3.5:1.

models.name: Network Effects
models.one_liner: Each additional user makes the product more valuable for all existing users — creating compounding competitive moats.
models.problem: Products compete on features alone without building the structural advantages that make switching costs and scale self-reinforcing.
models.how_it_works: Direct network effects: each new user directly benefits others (messaging apps). Indirect: each new user benefits a different user group (Uber — more drivers benefit riders). Data: more users → better ML models → better product → more users. Identify which type you have, then design to accelerate network density before competitors.
models.origin: Theodore Vail (AT&T, 1908) first observed the concept; formalized by Robert Metcalfe (Metcalfe's Law, 1980); popularized for startups by NFX and a16z in 2010s
models.scenario: A B2B SaaS founder is debating whether to charge enterprise customers higher fees early on, or subsidize onboarding to grow the user base faster. Her product is a supplier-buyer matching platform — more suppliers make it more valuable to buyers, and vice versa.
models.prompt: Map your two (or more) user groups and draw an arrow for each direction of value: who benefits when the other side grows? Then identify your biggest bottleneck side right now — and ask: what is the minimum subsidy or friction removal that unlocks density on that side first?
models.before_after: We should charge what we're worth and grow sustainably → The buyers won't pay until there are enough suppliers, so we need to subsidize supplier onboarding aggressively until we hit critical mass in two anchor cities.

models.name: Disruption Theory (Disruptive Innovation)
models.one_liner: Smaller companies overthrow incumbents by starting with simpler, cheaper products for overlooked customers, then moving upmarket.
models.problem: Incumbents dismiss threats that start at the low end of their market — until those competitors take the whole market.
models.how_it_works: Disruptors enter at the low end (or in a new market) with a simpler, cheaper solution that incumbents ignore (it's not profitable for them). Disruptors improve over time. When the product is 'good enough' for mainstream customers, the incumbent's core market collapses. Sustaining innovation (improving for existing customers) is not disruption.
models.origin: Clayton M. Christensen, Harvard Business School, 'The Innovator's Dilemma' (1997)
models.scenario: A product manager at a legacy enterprise HR software company notices a scrappy startup offering a free, stripped-down version of their core feature to small businesses. Leadership wants to ignore it because the startup's product is 'too basic for real customers.'
models.prompt: Ask: are those 'too basic' customers actually underserved — people we don't pursue because margins are thin? Then trace one step forward: if the startup improves 20% per year and our mainstream customers' bar for 'good enough' is fixed, at what year do they collide? Build that timeline explicitly.
models.before_after: They're targeting tiny SMBs we don't care about — they'll never compete with our enterprise suite → In three years their product crosses the 'good enough' threshold for our mid-market segment, and by then they'll have 40,000 reference customers we don't.

models.name: Technology Adoption Lifecycle (Crossing the Chasm)
models.one_liner: Innovations cross a market chasm between early adopters and the early majority — this gap kills most new products.
models.problem: Companies succeed with early adopters but fail to cross into mainstream markets because these groups have fundamentally different needs and reference systems.
models.how_it_works: The S-curve has segments: Innovators → Early Adopters → Early Majority → Late Majority → Laggards. The 'Chasm' lies between Early Adopters and Early Majority. To cross it: focus on a single 'bowling pin' niche, dominate it completely, then use it as a beachhead for adjacent segments.
models.origin: Everett Rogers, 'Diffusion of Innovations' (1962) — S-curve; Geoffrey Moore, 'Crossing the Chasm' (1991) — the Chasm
models.scenario: A health-tech startup's smart insulin pump has strong traction with endocrinologists and tech-savvy diabetic patients (early adopters), but sales have stalled for 8 months despite glowing reviews and press coverage.
models.prompt: List all the reasons your Early Adopters bought that your next 1,000 potential customers would NOT cite as reasons to buy. Then identify one specific niche — a single profession, condition severity, insurance type, or hospital system — where you can own 70%+ market share before expanding. What does 'dominating that bowling pin' look like in concrete numbers?
models.before_after: We have great reviews and word-of-mouth so growth should follow naturally → Early adopters buy on vision; the early majority buys on proven peer reference, so we need to dominate one segment completely — say, Type 1 adults at endocrinology clinics in three cities — before the mainstream will trust us.

models.name: Competitive Moat
models.one_liner: A durable structural advantage that prevents competitors from replicating your position and compressing your returns.
models.problem: Competitive advantages from product features or execution erode quickly; sustainable profitability requires structural barriers.
models.how_it_works: Seven types of moats (Hamilton Helmer's 7 Powers): Scale Economies, Network Effects, Switching Costs, Cornered Resource, Counter-Positioning, Branding, Process Power. Identify which moats your business has or can build. Moats compound — a business with multiple types is exponentially harder to attack.
models.origin: Warren Buffett coined the metaphor; Hamilton Helmer formalized seven types in '7 Powers: The Foundations of Business Strategy' (2016)
models.scenario: A solo founder just launched a profitable indie SaaS tool for freelance video editors. A larger competitor has noticed and could replicate the core feature in 60 days. The founder is trying to decide where to invest the next six months of development.
models.prompt: Score your business against each of the 7 Powers: Scale Economies, Network Effects, Switching Costs, Cornered Resource, Counter-Positioning, Branding, Process Power. Give each a 0-2 rating for how strong it is today. Then identify the ONE moat you could realistically deepen to a 2 in the next six months — and write the specific product or business decision that builds it.
models.before_after: I need to keep shipping features so my product stays better than theirs → My switching costs are a 1 today because editors can export everything; if I build deep integrations with their existing workflows and make their project history non-exportable, switching costs become a 2 and features become irrelevant.

models.name: Pre-mortem Analysis
models.one_liner: Before starting a project, imagine it has already failed catastrophically — then work backward to identify why.
models.problem: Teams are subject to groupthink and optimism bias during planning, failing to surface plausible failure modes.
models.how_it_works: At project kickoff, announce: 'It's 12 months from now and this project failed completely. What happened?' Everyone writes reasons independently (prevents anchoring), then shares. Identify the most plausible failure modes and build mitigations into the plan. Psychological safety is critical — it's safe to voice doubts.
models.origin: Gary Klein, cognitive psychologist, developed the pre-mortem technique in the 1990s; popularized by Daniel Kahneman in 'Thinking, Fast and Slow' (2011)
models.scenario: A hospital is rolling out a new electronic handoff system to reduce medication errors during nurse shift changes. The go-live is in three weeks, the team is energized, and the project looks on track. The project lead is running the final planning meeting.
models.prompt: Tell the team: 'It's six months from now and this rollout failed — nurses are ignoring the system, errors have increased, and leadership pulled the plug. Write down, individually and silently, the three most plausible reasons why.' Collect every reason, then rank them by likelihood and severity. For each top-three failure mode, assign one person to own a specific mitigation before go-live.
models.before_after: Everything looks on track so let's keep the momentum and launch on schedule → The pre-mortem surfaced that night-shift nurses have a 12-minute handoff window and the new system takes 9 — that specific friction would have caused workarounds; we delayed two weeks to add a quick-entry mode.

models.name: Law of Diminishing Returns
models.one_liner: Each additional unit of input yields progressively smaller incremental output beyond an optimal point.
models.problem: Resources are applied uniformly without recognizing that marginal returns degrade — overspending past the inflection point.
models.how_it_works: Map input vs. output. Returns rise steeply at first, flatten, then decline. The optimal input level is just before the plateau. Application: stop optimizing a feature past the point of diminishing returns; redistribute effort to higher-ROI areas. Closely related to Pareto — most gains come early.
models.origin: Anne Robert Jacques Turgot (1767) and Thomas Robert Malthus (1798) in economics; now cross-domain
models.scenario: A growth marketing manager has been running A/B tests on the company's landing page for four months. The first three tests produced 18%, 11%, and 6% conversion lifts. She's planning test 12 and her team is pushing for more headline and CTA variations.
models.prompt: Plot your last 8-10 tests on a simple chart: test number on the X axis, conversion lift on the Y axis. Draw the curve. Identify where it flattened. Then ask: if the next test yields a 1% lift, what is the actual revenue value — and what would that same engineering and analyst time yield if redirected to a completely untested part of the funnel?
models.before_after: We should keep testing the landing page because every improvement adds revenue → We're deep into diminishing returns on this page — the next 10 landing page tests might yield less total lift than one test on the checkout abandonment flow we've never touched.

models.name: Platform Business Model
models.one_liner: Build infrastructure that facilitates value exchange between two or more user groups — the platform captures value without producing it directly.
models.problem: Linear business models (make → sell) hit growth ceilings; platforms scale without proportional cost increases.
models.how_it_works: Identify two or more user groups with complementary needs (buyers/sellers, drivers/riders, developers/users). Build the infrastructure connecting them. Remove friction on both sides. Seed one side to attract the other (solving the chicken-and-egg problem). Revenue comes from facilitating interactions, not producing outputs.
models.origin: Network effects research in economics; popularized as a concept by Geoffrey Parker and Marshall Van Alstyne in 'Platform Revolution' (2016)
models.scenario: A former teacher is building a marketplace for tutors and K-12 students. She has 40 tutors signed up but almost no student bookings, and tutors are complaining the platform is a ghost town. She's about to spend her remaining runway on tutor acquisition.
models.prompt: Name the two sides of your platform and the chicken-and-egg dynamic between them. Then ask: which side is the 'hard side' that is expensive or difficult to acquire, and which is the 'easy side' that follows automatically once the other reaches density? Design one specific subsidy, event, or constraint that manufactures density on the hard side in a single geography or subject area first — before scaling.
models.before_after: I need more tutors so students have more choice → Students are the hard side — tutors sign up but won't stay without bookings; I should guarantee 5 paid sessions per month to 15 tutors in one city, fund it myself, and use that density to attract the first 50 student families before expanding.

connections.description: First Principles strips away assumptions to foundational truths; Issue Trees then structure those truths into a MECE decomposition for solving. Use First Principles to validate the branches you put in your Issue Tree.
Issue Trees are built by applying the MECE Principle at every node — MECE is the structural rule that makes Issue Trees valid.

Pre-Mortem is a structured team application of Inversion: instead of asking 'what would cause failure?' abstractly, the team imagines failure has already occurred and inverts from that concrete starting point.
Pre-Mortem surfaces concrete failure modes before launch; Regret Minimization calibrates emotional stakes. Together they prevent both cognitive and emotional decision traps.
Second-Order Thinking asks 'and then what?' verbally; CLDs formalize those downstream chains into visual feedback loops capturing nonlinear dynamics.
CLDs are composed of two primitive building blocks: Reinforcing Feedback Loops and Balancing Feedback Loops.
CLDs are composed of two primitive building blocks: Balancing Feedback Loops and Reinforcing Feedback Loops.
The Limits to Growth archetype begins with a Reinforcing Feedback Loop that eventually encounters a Balancing constraint — you cannot understand the archetype without the primitive loop.
Network Effects are a specific real-world manifestation of a Reinforcing Feedback Loop: more users → more value → more users.
The Flywheel Effect is a business-strategy framing of a Reinforcing Feedback Loop: strategic actions create momentum that compounds through the same cycle.
Compounding is the mathematical / financial expression of a Reinforcing Feedback Loop applied to a stock (money, knowledge, reputation) over time.
The Drifting Goals archetype occurs when a Balancing Loop intended to close a gap is gamed by lowering the goal instead of improving performance.
Both archetypes involve symptomatic fixes that undermine long-term solutions, but Shifting the Burden emphasizes dependency creation while Fixes That Fail emphasizes delayed blowback.

Goodhart's Law states that measures become corrupted when used as targets; the Cobra Effect is a vivid historical case where an incentive perversely increased the problem it was meant to solve — a concrete instance of Goodhart in action.

Campbell's Law is the social-science formulation of the same insight as Goodhart's Law, specifying that high-stakes use of indicators corrupts them — the two are parallel expressions of the same phenomenon in different domains.
The Iceberg Model provides a four-level map (events → patterns → structures → mental models) of where root causes live; Five Whys is the drilling technique used to traverse those levels.
Five Whys is one specific technique within the broader Root Cause Analysis discipline, which also includes Fishbone Diagrams and fault trees.
The Fishbone Diagram is a visual tool within Root Cause Analysis, structuring potential causes across standard categories to reveal the root.
Root Cause Analysis is the analytical phase that informs the Plan step of PDCA — you need to understand root causes before designing a corrective change.
PDSA is Deming's preferred refinement of PDCA, substituting 'Study' for 'Check' to emphasize learning over verification — both serve iterative improvement but with different epistemological emphasis.
Build-Measure-Learn is the Lean Startup variant of PDCA, optimized for product validation under uncertainty rather than process improvement in stable systems.
MVP is the artifact produced in the Build phase of Build-Measure-Learn — it is the minimum experiment needed to generate validated learning.
The Double Diamond is a spatial visualization of the Design Thinking process, making its two diverge-converge cycles (problem space + solution space) structurally explicit.
The Empathy Map is a specific tool used in the Empathize phase of Design Thinking to build a structured understanding of user context.
JTBD reframes what problem Design Thinking should be solving — defining the job the user wants done before the empathy/ideation phases begin.
Each diamond in the Double Diamond model is a diverge-converge cycle — Divergent/Convergent Thinking is the cognitive mechanism the model formalizes.
CPS explicitly alternates divergent and convergent phases at each of its six stages — the cognitive pattern is the engine of the entire model.
Brainstorming is the pure divergent phase — it defers all convergent judgment to a later step, directly enacting divergent thinking.
Reverse Brainstorming applies the Inversion mental model to standard Brainstorming, generating ideas about how to cause the problem then flipping them into solutions.
Reverse Brainstorming is a creativity technique that operationalizes Inversion — it is Inversion applied in a group ideation context.
SCAMPER provides seven specific lateral moves (Substitute, Combine, Adapt…) that systematize what Lateral Thinking does intuitively — breaking dominant patterns through structured provocations.
Synectics is a structured group creativity method built entirely on four types of analogical thinking, making Analogical Thinking the foundational cognitive operation.
The Zwicky Box generates all possible combinations across independent dimensions; MECE ensures those dimensions are properly non-overlapping and exhaustive before the matrix is built.
First Principles strips away false assumptions; Constraint Relaxation temporarily removes assumed constraints — together they clear the solution space of inherited limitations.
Abstraction Laddering moves up (Why?) to reframe the problem space and down (How?) to generate alternatives; Issue Trees then structure the decomposed problem for systematic solving.
Abstraction Laddering is a structured technique for achieving the cognitive shift that Reframing describes — moving up or down the abstraction hierarchy to see the problem differently.
Cynefin's 'Complex' domain describes precisely the territory that CAS theory analyzes — the framework tells you when you're in that domain; CAS theory explains what to do there.
Wicked Problems are a subset of the problems that live in Cynefin's Complex or Chaotic domains — the Cynefin Framework provides the classification system that identifies a problem as wicked.
The Cynefin Framework classifies the decision environment; OODA Loop provides the action cycle optimized for Chaotic and Complex domains where speed of iteration matters most.
Decision Trees are computational structures for calculating Expected Value across branching paths — each leaf is an outcome weighted by probability, summed along each branch.
Expected Value is the mathematical operationalization of Probabilistic Thinking — it forces explicit probability estimates and multiplies them by magnitude to compare options.
Bayesian Updating provides the formal mechanism for how probabilities should change in Probabilistic Thinking as new evidence arrives — it is dynamic probabilistic reasoning.
Planning Fallacy describes the bias of inside-view optimism; Reference Class Forecasting is the debiasing technique that corrects it by anchoring on outside-view historical base rates.
Reference Class Forecasting provides the prior from historical data; Bayesian Updating then combines that prior with inside-view project-specific evidence to form a calibrated forecast.

Margin of Safety is a defensive buffer against downside; Antifragility goes further by structuring exposure so that volatility itself becomes a source of gain — they address different points on the robustness-to-optionality spectrum.
Black Swans are the events that destroy fragile systems; Antifragility is the design principle that transforms Black Swan exposure into upside — Taleb developed both concepts in tandem.

Black Swans are the qualitative concept; Power Laws/Fat Tails provide the statistical framework explaining why they occur — heavy-tailed distributions make extreme events far more probable than Gaussian models suggest.
The Pareto Principle is an empirical observation of power-law distribution — the 80/20 rule is a special case of the broader mathematical phenomenon of power-law scaling.
The Tipping Point is the moment in a Diffusion of Innovation S-curve where adoption crosses the threshold that triggers exponential spread — it is a specific feature of the S-curve.
Crossing the Chasm refines the S-curve by identifying a dangerous gap between Early Adopters and Early Majority that the original Diffusion of Innovation model smooths over.

Hysteresis is the physics/engineering term for the same phenomenon as Path Dependence — the current state depends on history. Hysteresis is used in physical systems; Path Dependence in social/economic ones.
Panarchy is the ecological theory explaining how resilience is maintained across scales through adaptive cycles — the Resilience concept is formalized within Panarchy's r-K-Ω-α model.
Tragedy of the Commons diagnoses why shared resources fail; Ostrom's Design Principles provide the empirically-derived solution — the eight conditions under which commons actually succeed.
Tragedy of the Commons is a specific spatial/resource case of the broader Collective Action Problem — rational individual behavior leading to collective suboptimality.
Build a CLD first to map the system's structure; then apply Meadows' Leverage Points hierarchy to identify where in that map intervention will be most effective.
Stock-and-Flow diagrams make the accumulations and rates explicit; CLDs show the feedback structure — together they form a complete system dynamics model.
The Dunning-Kruger Effect explains why people don't know the boundaries of their competence; Circle of Competence is the discipline of deliberately mapping and respecting those boundaries.
The Feynman Technique is an operational debiasing tool for the Dunning-Kruger Effect — attempting plain-language explanation exposes the gaps that overconfident knowers cannot see.
The Iceberg Model maps the four levels where assumptions live; Double-Loop Learning is the learning process that reaches below the surface to question those underlying structures and mental models.
System 1/2 describes the two cognitive modes; Metacognition is the self-monitoring capacity that notices when System 1 is running and decides whether to engage System 2.
ZPD defines the learning sweet spot between known and unknown; Deliberate Practice is the method for continuously operating in that zone through targeted, feedback-driven challenge.
The Forgetting Curve shows when memories decay; Spaced Repetition schedules reviews precisely when decay is about to occur — the technique is designed as the antidote to the curve.

Spaced Repetition optimizes the timing of retrieval; the Testing Effect shows that retrieval itself (not re-reading) is what strengthens memory — they are the timing and mechanism of the same learning process.

Deliberate Practice identifies targeted weakness work; Interleaving is a practice schedule that mixes topics to force discriminative retrieval — together they accelerate skill acquisition beyond blocked practice.

Kolb's cycle describes how experience becomes knowledge through reflection and conceptualization; Double-Loop Learning adds a meta-level where the governing assumptions of the cycle itself are questioned.
Bloom's Taxonomy shows that teaching others sits at the highest cognitive level (Synthesis/Create); Feynman Technique operationalizes that level as a self-assessment tool for any learner.
Growth Mindset is the belief system that makes Deliberate Practice possible — without believing ability is developable, people avoid the productive difficulty that deliberate practice requires.
Confirmation Bias is the failure to update properly on disconfirming evidence; Bayesian Updating is the normative corrective — the mathematical rule that forces symmetric updating on all evidence.
Availability Heuristic causes overweighting of vivid memorable examples; Reference Class Forecasting corrects this by anchoring on full statistical distributions rather than recalled instances.
Survivorship Bias pollutes the visible sample by hiding failures; Reference Class Forecasting must explicitly include failures in its base rate to generate unbiased priors.
Narrative Fallacy creates false causal explanations for random sequences; Bayesian Updating demands explicit probability assignments that resist the post-hoc story-fitting that narratives enable.

Hindsight Bias makes past failures seem inevitable; Pre-Mortem exploits that same imaginative capacity prospectively — using the brain's tendency to construct post-hoc inevitability to surface real risks before the fact.
Framing Effect shows that how problems are presented systematically changes decisions; Reframing is the deliberate technique of exploiting that same mechanism to open new solution spaces.
These are the same phenomenon described from different angles: Sunk Cost Fallacy is the bias (descriptive), Sunk Cost Awareness is the debiasing discipline (prescriptive).
Loss Aversion is the central empirical finding of Kahneman and Tversky's Prospect Theory — the roughly 2:1 asymmetry between loss pain and gain pleasure is the engine of the entire theory.
Overconfidence Bias explains why our estimates are systematically too narrow; Margin of Safety is the engineering discipline that compensates for that overconfidence by building explicit buffers.
Planning Fallacy describes the bias toward optimistic time/cost estimates; SMART Goals' 'Achievable' and 'Time-bound' criteria are partial correctives — forcing realistic scoping before commitment.

Status Quo Bias creates excessive inertia on reversible decisions; the Type 1/Type 2 framework corrects this by freeing you to move fast on reversible (Type 2) decisions that status quo bias would cause you to overthink.

Ladder of Inference maps the cognitive steps where Confirmation Bias enters — specifically at data selection (rung 2) and meaning-making (rung 4) — providing a structural diagnosis of where the bias operates.
The Decision Matrix is a System 2 tool — it forces the slow, deliberate, analytical mode onto decisions that System 1 would otherwise shortcut through emotional or heuristic processing.
The SCQA Framework is a specific narrative structure for building the setup that leads to BLUF's conclusion-first delivery — SCQA answers 'how do I set up the pyramid?'

Monroe's Motivated Sequence provides the temporal structure (Attention → Action) while the Rhetorical Triangle provides the three persuasive channels — together they cover both the order and the modes of persuasion.
Cialdini developed both: Pre-Suasion is his later discovery that what happens before the influence attempt matters as much as the 7 Principles during it — they are sequentially integrated.

Steel-Manning builds the best counter-argument for personal reasoning; Inoculation Theory uses weakened counter-arguments to pre-emptively protect audiences against persuasion — both work by engaging opposing arguments before the critical moment.
BATNA/ZOPA define the positional parameters of a negotiation; Principled Negotiation provides the process for expanding the pie within those parameters by focusing on interests rather than positions.

Both frameworks center on surfacing needs and emotions beneath stated positions — NVC uses a four-part structure for collaborative contexts; Tactical Empathy uses mirroring and labeling for adversarial ones.

These are the same phenomenon in different domains: Anchoring Effect (cognitive-bias) is the descriptive observation; Anchoring (negotiation) is the prescriptive exploitation of that bias to gain advantage.

The Johari Window shows what information is hidden between self and others; Psychological Safety is the team condition that makes it safe to expand the Open quadrant by reducing Hidden and Blind areas.
Radical Candor defines the management philosophy (care personally + challenge directly); SBI provides the tactical communication structure for delivering that challenging feedback without judgment.

The Five Dysfunctions pyramid's base (Absence of Trust) is precisely what Psychological Safety addresses — Edmondson's research on safety operationalizes and measures what Lencioni diagnoses narratively.
ZPD defines the learning zone relative to capability; Situational Leadership maps management style to that same capability-commitment matrix, applying ZPD principles to leadership.

SCARF provides the neurological mechanism for why Psychological Safety matters — the Status, Certainty, Autonomy, Relatedness, and Fairness domains are the specific threat triggers that Psychological Safety neutralizes.

The Trust Equation makes Self-Orientation the dominant denominator (high self-orientation destroys trust); Radical Candor's 'care personally' dimension is the behavioral expression of low self-orientation.
TA diagnoses ego-state mismatches that cause communication breakdowns; NVC provides the language pattern that moves both parties toward the Adult-Adult transaction TA identifies as productive.
Bottleneck Analysis is the analytical step within TOC's five focusing steps — TOC is the complete management philosophy; Bottleneck Analysis is the identification phase.
Drum-Buffer-Rope is the scheduling algorithm derived from TOC — it operationalizes the constraint-focus principle into production planning rules.

CPM identifies the longest chain of task dependencies; TOC identifies the binding capacity constraint — in projects (Critical Chain), the combination protects the critical path with buffer management.
Kanban's WIP limits are a practical implementation of TOC's principle of subordinating everything to the constraint — limiting work-in-progress prevents building inventory ahead of bottlenecks.
Agile/Scrum provides the team process and cadence (sprints, ceremonies); Build-Measure-Learn provides the learning philosophy that should guide what gets built in each sprint.

The North Star Metric is the ultimate direction; OKRs cascade that direction into quarterly objectives with measurable results — the North Star prevents OKRs from optimizing locally at the expense of the overall mission.

Porter's Five Forces systematically maps the external competitive environment (informing the Opportunities and Threats quadrants); SWOT then combines that external view with internal Strengths and Weaknesses.
PESTLE provides structured macro-environmental scanning that populates the Opportunities and Threats cells of SWOT — PESTLE is the analytical input; SWOT is the strategic synthesis.

Porter's Five Forces analyzes competitive intensity within an industry; Blue Ocean Strategy prescribes creating new market space where those five forces are irrelevant — it is a strategic response to unattractive Five Forces analysis.
Disruption Theory explains the incumbent's strategic failure to respond; the Chasm model explains the innovator's execution challenge — together they map both sides of a market disruption.
Wardley Mapping adds evolutionary maturity as a second dimension to Value Chain Analysis — it asks not just 'what creates value?' but 'how mature is each component and where is it heading?'
The BCG Matrix diagnoses the current portfolio; the Ansoff Matrix prescribes growth vectors — together they answer 'what do we have?' and 'where should we go?'
The Lean Canvas is the hypothesis document that specifies what the MVP should test — you identify the riskiest assumption in the canvas, then build the MVP to validate it.
JTBD provides the deep customer motivation insight that should anchor the Problem and Customer Segments boxes of the Lean Canvas — it ensures the canvas reflects real demand.

Conway's Law predicts that systems mirror organizational communication structures (a subset of 7S); McKinsey 7S provides the full map of organizational elements that must align — Structure, Systems, Staff, Style, Skills, Strategy, Shared Values.

Platform Business Models are the organizational structure designed to capture Network Effects — the platform exists specifically to aggregate users whose interactions create the compounding value described by Metcalfe's Law.
Preferential Attachment is the generative mechanism explaining how Network Effects produce winner-take-all hub structures — it shows why network effects tend toward concentration.
Network Effects are one of the most powerful sources of Competitive Moat — the self-reinforcing value growth makes the advantage structurally durable against competitors.
Switching costs and lock-in effects are a key category of Competitive Moat — Path Dependence explains the structural mechanism by which customers cannot easily leave even when alternatives exist.
The Revenue Streams and Cost Structure sections of the Lean Canvas require unit economics analysis — you cannot validate a business model without understanding per-customer contribution margin.
Parkinson's Law explains why unstructured time gets wasted; Time Blocking is the countermeasure — assigning specific work to specific hours prevents work from expanding to fill available slack.

Deep Work is the deliberate practice discipline (scheduled blocks, distraction elimination); Flow State is the psychological optimal experience that Deep Work creates conditions for — one is the discipline, the other is the reward.

The Pomodoro Technique provides time structure that protects against interruption; however, its frequent breaks can disrupt Flow — skilled practitioners adapt interval length to extend flow states once entered.

The Eisenhower Matrix identifies what is Important but not Urgent (Q2 work); Eat the Frog prescribes doing the most important/dreaded task first — together they align daily execution with strategic priority.
The Two-Minute Rule is a specific decision rule within GTD's processing step — if a captured item takes less than two minutes, do it now rather than organizing it into the system.
GTD's context lists (e.g., '@phone', '@computer') are a form of Batching — grouping similar tasks by context to enable efficient sequential execution.

The Hard Choice Model categorizes by impact × comparability; the Type 1/Type 2 framework categorizes by reversibility — together they create a richer decision-routing system that matches effort to both stakes and reversibility.

The WRAP Framework's 'Widen options' and 'Reality-test assumptions' steps directly target Confirmation Bias — the framework is essentially a structured debiasing protocol for the four decision biases.
Both models use temporal distancing to reduce present-emotional bias in decisions — 10-10-10 uses three specific time horizons; Regret Minimization uses a single extreme projection (age 80).

Scenario Planning generates multiple futures for strategic hedging; Pre-Mortem focuses that same imaginative capacity on a single failure scenario to extract actionable risk mitigations before launch.
Scenario Planning builds narratives of alternative futures; Second-Order Thinking provides the recursive 'and then what?' mechanism for extending those scenarios beyond first-level projections.

Both tools solve the 'wrong problem' trap by moving to more abstract formulations — Concept Fan explicitly builds multiple branches at each level; Abstraction Laddering uses a single vertical Why?/How? axis.
Both are radial visual decomposition tools, but Mind Maps are associative and non-MECE (for exploration), while Issue Trees are logical and strictly MECE (for exhaustive problem-solving).

Bloom's Taxonomy defines the levels of cognitive engagement with knowledge; Munger's Latticework prescribes building a diverse cross-domain library that can be applied at the synthesis/evaluation levels.
Both are reflective learning frameworks that move from experience through meaning to action — What/So What/Now What is a simplified three-step variant of Kolb's four-stage cycle.

First Principles builds up from foundational truths; Occam's Razor prunes back to the simplest sufficient explanation — together they prevent both under-thinking (accepting convention) and over-engineering (adding unnecessary complexity).

Fundamental Attribution Error causes us to attribute others' negative behavior to malice or character; Hanlon's Razor is the corrective heuristic — assume incompetence before malice to avoid that attribution error.

Goodhart's Law is a specific application of Map/Territory confusion: the metric is a map of the underlying reality, and when it becomes a target (is treated as the territory), the map-territory distinction collapses destructively.

Gall's Law prescribes evolutionary design from working simple systems; CAS theory explains why — complex adaptive systems cannot be designed top-down because their behavior is emergent from component interactions.
Emergence is the defining property of Complex Adaptive Systems — the system-level behaviors that cannot be predicted from or reduced to the properties of individual components.
Self-Organization is a core mechanism of Complex Adaptive Systems — ordered structures arise from local agent interactions without central control, which is what makes CAS systems CAS systems.
Chaos Theory is the mathematical study of extreme nonlinear sensitivity to initial conditions — nonlinearity is the necessary structural property that makes chaotic behavior possible.
Chaos Theory explains the unpredictability of trajectories; Strange Attractors show that despite trajectory unpredictability, chaotic systems remain bounded within a recognizable geometric structure.

Unintended Consequences describes the phenomenon; Second-Order Thinking is the preventive cognitive practice — systematically asking 'and then what?' before acting to surface consequences before they materialize.
Jevons Paradox is a specific, historically well-documented instance of Unintended Consequences — an efficiency improvement producing the opposite of its intended reduction in total resource use.

Braess's Paradox is a specific mathematical instance of Unintended Consequences in network systems where adding capacity degrades performance due to individual optimization conflicting with system optimum.

Normal Accidents Theory applies CAS insights to safety-critical systems: in tightly coupled complex systems, multi-point failures are statistically inevitable — a consequence of emergent system behavior.

Forrester identified that complex systems behave counterintuitively — Meadows' Leverage Points hierarchy is the systematic answer to that insight, mapping where non-obvious high-leverage interventions actually exist.

Lindy Effect advises trusting knowledge and technologies that have survived long — applying it as a filter when defining your Circle of Competence helps identify which frameworks have proven durable versus merely fashionable.

Both are structured multi-step creative problem-solving processes that alternate divergent and convergent phases — Productive Thinking Model uses six named steps; CPS uses six distinct stages with different framing.
Both are structured group decision-making processes that counteract social pressure biases — Delphi uses iterative anonymous rounds; Six Thinking Hats uses simultaneous parallel perspectives.

The Evaporating Cloud surfaces the hidden assumption behind conflicting requirements; Principled Negotiation uses interests-not-positions thinking to find the same hidden win-win space — both dissolve apparent conflicts rather than compromise.
SPIN Selling is an application of Socratic questioning in a sales context — it uses guided questions to help buyers discover their own needs rather than the seller asserting solutions.

Theory U's four listening levels begin at Downloading (the opposite of active listening) and progress through Active Listening toward Generative Listening — it provides a developmental hierarchy that places Active Listening in a larger framework.

Mendelow's Matrix tells you who to engage and how; SCARF tells you what those stakeholders' neurological threat triggers are — combining them enables both strategic targeting and emotionally intelligent engagement.

Both are narrative structure templates — the Hero's Journey provides a rich twelve-stage arc for complex stories; Story Spine is a minimal fill-in-the-blank template for quick well-formed story generation.
The Overton Window describes the current range of acceptable ideas; Inoculation Theory provides a mechanism for both defending that window against manipulation and deliberately shifting it over time.
The Affect Heuristic is a specific System 1 operation — emotional state is used as information input to judgment, bypassing the deliberate evidential analysis of System 2.
Base Rate Fallacy describes the cognitive tendency to ignore base rates; Reference Class Forecasting is the structured technique that forces base rate incorporation into estimates.

The Conjunction Fallacy is caused by the Representativeness Heuristic — the detailed scenario feels more representative of the prototype than the broader category, so it incorrectly seems more probable.

False Consensus overestimates how many people share your views; Ingroup Bias shows you also favor those who do share your views — together they create echo chambers where your group seems like the rational majority.

Availability Cascade is a societal-scale amplification of the Illusory Truth Effect — repeated public discussion makes ideas feel truer (Illusory Truth), and that perceived truth further amplifies discussion in a cascade.

The Peak-End Rule shows that endings disproportionately shape memory of an experience; Monroe's Motivated Sequence ends with an Action step designed to be a strong, memorable close — leveraging peak-end dynamics.

The Zeigarnik Effect explains why incomplete tasks occupy mental bandwidth (open loops); GTD's external capture system closes those loops, eliminating the cognitive load the Zeigarnik Effect predicts.

The IKEA Effect is an extension of the Endowment Effect — we over-value things we own, and even more so things we partially built ourselves, because the construction effort amplifies the ownership sense.

The Ben Franklin Effect shows that doing someone a favor increases liking through rationalization; this operates through the Commitment/Consistency principle — once you've acted in favor of someone, you maintain that consistent self-image.

The Serial Position Effect shows primacy and recency dominate memory; BLUF exploits the primacy effect by placing the conclusion first — the most important information occupies the strongest memory position.

Loss Aversion is a specific domain application of the broader Negativity Bias — the general asymmetric weighting of negative over positive stimuli manifests in economic contexts as the ~2:1 loss/gain sensitivity ratio.

Planning Fallacy is the project/time-estimate manifestation of the broader Optimism Bias — the same unrealistic positive expectations that make us believe we're less likely to experience bad events also make us underestimate project duration.
Mere Exposure Effect increases liking through repetition — this is the psychological mechanism underlying Cialdini's Liking principle when applied to repeated brand/message contact.

Curse of Knowledge makes experts unable to communicate simply; Feynman Technique is the practice that forces experts to confront that curse by requiring plain-language explanation to an imagined novice.

Both are errors about random sequences — Gambler's Fallacy expects reversion after a streak (the sequence is 'due'); Hot-Hand Fallacy expects continuation of a streak (momentum is 'real') — opposite errors from the same misunderstanding of independence.

Google Effect shows we fail to encode externally-stored information; GTD exploits this intentionally — by externalizing commitments, it frees cognitive RAM, treating the system as intended cognitive offloading rather than memory failure.

Impact Bias causes us to overestimate how intensely future events will affect us; 10-10-10 corrects this by revealing that at 10 years, even major decisions lose their emotional charge — reducing fear-based inaction.

Plan Continuation Bias causes dangerous persistence with original plans despite new disconfirming information; the OODA Loop's Observe-Orient cycle is a structural forcing function for continuous re-assessment that combats this bias.

Backfire Effect shows that direct counter-evidence can entrench beliefs; Inoculation Theory sidesteps this by pre-exposing people to weakened arguments before they form strong attachments — avoiding the defensive entrenchment.
Ethos (credibility) is the persuasive channel that exploits Authority Bias — establishing speaker authority activates the cognitive shortcut that makes people defer to credible sources.
The Bandwagon Effect is the cognitive mechanism underlying Cialdini's Social Proof principle — we adopt beliefs/behaviors because others do, especially under uncertainty.

Recency Bias over-weights recent observations; Bayesian Updating corrects this by weighting new evidence proportionally to its likelihood ratio, preventing any single recent observation from dominating the posterior.

Halo Effect generalizes positive traits across unrelated dimensions; Fundamental Attribution Error attributes those traits to stable character rather than situation — together they produce systematically biased person-assessments.

Availability Cascades are a mechanism for shifting the Overton Window — repeated public discussion makes previously extreme ideas feel normal and mainstream, expanding the window of acceptable discourse.

The Pareto Principle shows that 20% of inputs drive 80% of outputs; the Law of Diminishing Returns explains why — each additional unit of input yields less than the previous, making the first inputs disproportionately valuable.

The Hedgehog Concept (passion + best-at + economic engine) is an organizational version of Circle of Competence — it defines the intersection where an organization has genuine, defensible expertise worth concentrating on.

North Star Metric deliberately selects a metric that best captures customer value rather than a narrow business output — it is an attempt to choose a measure resistant to Goodhart's Law by anchoring on genuine value creation.

Issue Trees formalize Problem Decomposition by adding the MECE constraint — Problem Decomposition is the general act; Issue Trees are the structured, logically rigorous methodology for doing it correctly.

Working Backwards is constructive Inversion — instead of asking 'what causes failure?', you ask 'what defines success?' and reason back. Both start from an end state and work backward to present conditions.

The Veil of Ignorance helps design fair rules by removing positional self-interest; Principled Negotiation achieves similar fairness in live negotiations by using objective criteria rather than positional bargaining.
TRIZ identifies 40 inventive principles for resolving engineering contradictions — many of those principles involve specific forms of constraint relaxation, segmentation, or dimension change.

Deep Work creates conditions for sustained focused thinking; Focused vs. Diffuse Thinking shows that deliberately alternating with relaxed diffuse states (walks, sleep) produces better insights than unbroken focus — the two are compatible at different timescales.

Learning Pyramid shows that active/teaching methods produce highest retention; Deliberate Practice is the methodology that operates at those highest-retention levels through feedback-driven challenge.

Survivorship Bias makes successful incumbents seem invulnerable (we see survivors); Disruption Theory explains the structural mechanism by which those seemingly strong incumbents are systematically vulnerable to low-end attack.
Parkinson's Law explains why tasks expand to fill available time; Pomodoro Technique counters this by imposing artificial time constraints (25-minute sprints) that compress work into focused units.

Both are assertive communication scripts for delivering feedback or addressing conflict without triggering defensiveness — DESC covers Describe/Express/Specify/Consequences; SBI covers Situation/Behavior/Impact with different framing.
I-Messages are the communication form that NVC operationalizes — NVC provides the full framework (observation, feeling, need, request) of which I-Messages are the feeling-expression component.

Authentic Leadership requires deep self-knowledge and transparent values; the Johari Window is a tool for developing that self-awareness and reducing the Hidden and Unknown quadrants through feedback and disclosure.

Servant Leadership puts followers' needs first; this creates the caring environment that is a precondition for Psychological Safety — leaders who are seen as servants rather than threats make it safe to speak up.
